item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Flow‐Controlled Living Anionic Monoadditional Reaction Initiated by Flash‐Generated Benzophenone Dianions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Flash generation of metal benzophenone dianions using metal naphthalenides under microflow conditions has enabled selective anionic monoaddition of methacrylates giving chain‐ or ring‐type monoadducts and sequence‐defined oligomers within seconds.
The fast and selective generation of metal benzophenone dianions was achieved under microflow conditions using metal naphthalenides (MNp, M = Li, K) as reductants. By adjusting the stoichiometric ratio between benzophenone derivatives and LiNp, lithium benzophenone dianions were generated, which underwent highly selective 1:1 monoaddition with methacrylates within seconds. Flash‐flow mixing minimized side reactions such as the generation of benzophenone radical anions and nucleophilic ester cleavage, affording chain‐type monoaddition products in up to 96% yield. The method exhibited broad substrate scope toward various benzophenone derivatives and methacrylates. Furthermore, end functionalization and the synthesis of multicomponent coupling products were achieved through end‐capping reactions with diverse electrophiles. Sequential monomer introduction enabled the synthesis of sequence‐defined oligomers. Mechanistic studies revealed that the selective formation between chain‐ and ring‐type monoadducts was governed by the reactivity of the electrophile and the nature of the metal counterion. Thus, this method provides a versatile synthetic platform for the selective generation of benzophenone dianions and anionic monoadditions under microflow conditions.
item.content:encoded:
Flash generation of metal benzophenone dianions using metal naphthalenides under microflow conditions has enabled selective anionic monoaddition of methacrylates giving chain- or ring-type monoadducts and sequence-defined oligomers within seconds.


The fast and selective generation of metal benzophenone dianions was achieved under microflow conditions using metal naphthalenides (MNp, M = Li, K) as reductants. By adjusting the stoichiometric ratio between benzophenone derivatives and LiNp, lithium benzophenone dianions were generated, which underwent highly selective 1:1 monoaddition with methacrylates within seconds. Flash-flow mixing minimized side reactions such as the generation of benzophenone radical anions and nucleophilic ester cleavage, affording chain-type monoaddition products in up to 96% yield. The method exhibited broad substrate scope toward various benzophenone derivatives and methacrylates. Furthermore, end functionalization and the synthesis of multicomponent coupling products were achieved through end-capping reactions with diverse electrophiles. Sequential monomer introduction enabled the synthesis of sequence-defined oligomers. Mechanistic studies revealed that the selective formation between chain- and ring-type monoadducts was governed by the reactivity of the electrophile and the nature of the metal counterion. Thus, this method provides a versatile synthetic platform for the selective generation of benzophenone dianions and anionic monoadditions under microflow conditions.
item.dc:creator: Dong‐eun Yoo, Kazuhiro Okamoto, Aiichiro Nagaki
item.dc:title: Flow‐Controlled Living Anionic Monoadditional Reaction Initiated by Flash‐Generated Benzophenone Dianions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Enantiospecific Acid‐Catalysed Hydroperoxidation of Scalemic Tertiary Benzylic Alcohols Facilitating a Concise Asymmetric Synthesis of Yingzhaosu C
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Asymmetric synthesis of antimalarial yingzhaosu C was achieved in 4–5 steps by asymmetric Grignard synthesis of (R)‐gossonorol followed by novel enantiospecific hydroperoxidation.
We report a concise asymmetric synthesis of antimalarial yingzhaosu C in 59% ee. This 4‐ or 5‐step synthesis (depending on starting material) uses a novel route to scalemic tertiary benzylic hydroperoxides through the unprecedented enantiospecific hydroperoxidation of enantioenriched alcohols. To our knowledge, this is also the first report of the use in organic synthesis of solutions of hydrogen peroxide in cyclopentyl methyl ether. We propose that the hydroperoxidation occurs by a concerted process involving a partial positive charge at the reaction site. We believe that the availability of this synthetic route to yingzhaosu C may encourage exploration of the antimalarial properties, both of it and of its derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
Asymmetric synthesis of antimalarial yingzhaosu C was achieved in 4–5 steps by asymmetric Grignard synthesis of (R)-gossonorol followed by novel enantiospecific hydroperoxidation.


We report a concise asymmetric synthesis of antimalarial yingzhaosu C in 59% ee. This 4- or 5-step synthesis (depending on starting material) uses a novel route to scalemic tertiary benzylic hydroperoxides through the unprecedented enantiospecific hydroperoxidation of enantioenriched alcohols. To our knowledge, this is also the first report of the use in organic synthesis of solutions of hydrogen peroxide in cyclopentyl methyl ether. We propose that the hydroperoxidation occurs by a concerted process involving a partial positive charge at the reaction site. We believe that the availability of this synthetic route to yingzhaosu C may encourage exploration of the antimalarial properties, both of it and of its derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Saranna E. Kavanagh, Claudio Monasterolo, Declan G. Gilheany
item.dc:title: Enantiospecific Acid‐Catalysed Hydroperoxidation of Scalemic Tertiary Benzylic Alcohols Facilitating a Concise Asymmetric Synthesis of Yingzhaosu C

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: An Update on Total Synthesis and Structural Modifications of Glabridin
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Glabridin, a prenylated isoflavonoid isolated from licorice root, has gained attention for its diverse biological properties. This review provides a comprehensive overview of reported synthetic methodologies for glabridin covering several patent disclosure and academic publications, focusing on both racemic and enantioselective approaches that may be useful for synthetic chemistry to develop better and robust synthetic method for glabridin.
Natural products continue to play a pivotal role in medicinal chemistry due to their remarkable structural diversity and wide‐ranging pharmacological activities. Licorice, a well‐known traditional Chinese medicinal herb, is a rich source of bioactive compounds. Among these, glabridin, a prenylated isoflavonoid isolated from licorice root, has attracted considerable attention owing to its diverse biological properties. Total synthesis serves as a critical platform for advancing organic chemistry by enabling the construction of structurally complex natural products from simple starting materials. Over the years, several multistep synthetic strategies have been developed for the preparation of glabridin, commonly originating from simple phenol/resorcinol or coumarin derivatives. These approaches typically involve key transformations such as condensation, cyclization, and reduction to construct the isoflavanone framework. This review provides a comprehensive overview of reported synthetic methodologies for glabridin, with particular emphasis on both racemic and enantioselective approaches. Overall, it summarizes literature reports and patents related to the total synthesis and derivatization of glabridin, which may be useful for synthetic chemists to develop better and more robust synthetic method for glabridin.
item.content:encoded:
Glabridin, a prenylated isoflavonoid isolated from licorice root, has gained attention for its diverse biological properties. This review provides a comprehensive overview of reported synthetic methodologies for glabridin covering several patent disclosure and academic publications, focusing on both racemic and enantioselective approaches that may be useful for synthetic chemistry to develop better and robust synthetic method for glabridin.


Natural products continue to play a pivotal role in medicinal chemistry due to their remarkable structural diversity and wide-ranging pharmacological activities. Licorice, a well-known traditional Chinese medicinal herb, is a rich source of bioactive compounds. Among these, glabridin, a prenylated isoflavonoid isolated from licorice root, has attracted considerable attention owing to its diverse biological properties. Total synthesis serves as a critical platform for advancing organic chemistry by enabling the construction of structurally complex natural products from simple starting materials. Over the years, several multistep synthetic strategies have been developed for the preparation of glabridin, commonly originating from simple phenol/resorcinol or coumarin derivatives. These approaches typically involve key transformations such as condensation, cyclization, and reduction to construct the isoflavanone framework. This review provides a comprehensive overview of reported synthetic methodologies for glabridin, with particular emphasis on both racemic and enantioselective approaches. Overall, it summarizes literature reports and patents related to the total synthesis and derivatization of glabridin, which may be useful for synthetic chemists to develop better and more robust synthetic method for glabridin.

item.dc:title: An Update on Total Synthesis and Structural Modifications of Glabridin

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: (3,9)Carbazolophane – An Effective Chiral Dopant for Achiral Polyfluorene
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This study explores an alternative Suzuki–Cadogan synthesis of carbazolophane (Czp), yielding a previously undescribed (3,9)‐isomer. The planar chiral molecule is able to induce a structural reorganization in achiral polyfluorene (PFO) upon thermal annealing. This significantly enhances the chiroptical properties, broadening the scope of effective chiral dopants for dissymmetric emission from organic semiconductors.
Carbazolophane (Czp) is a well‐known, electron‐rich paracyclophane (PCP) molecule that allows to introduce planar chirality in organic emitters, notably those displaying thermally activated delayed fluorescence. In an attempt to optimize the preparation of Czp, an alternative synthetic route is explored, following a Suzuki–Cadogan sequence, affording a previously undescribed (3,9)‐isomer. The shifted anchoring points give rise to clear structural and spectroscopic differences. The two (3,9)Czp enantiomers are successfully resolved and their chiroptical properties are determined in toluene solution. Considering the growing interest in π‐conjugated polymers for circularly polarized optoelectronics, due to their ability to form chiral helical assemblies and thereby realize strong chiroptical activity, (3,9)Czp is then explored as a chiral small‐molecule dopant for achiral poly(9,9‐dioctylfluorene) (PFO). Polymer films containing 20 wt% of the Sp‐enantiomer display the strongest chiral amplification (following thermal treatment), reaching a gabs value of 3.0 × 10−2.
item.content:encoded:
This study explores an alternative Suzuki–Cadogan synthesis of carbazolophane (Czp), yielding a previously undescribed (3,9)-isomer. The planar chiral molecule is able to induce a structural reorganization in achiral polyfluorene (PFO) upon thermal annealing. This significantly enhances the chiroptical properties, broadening the scope of effective chiral dopants for dissymmetric emission from organic semiconductors.


Carbazolophane (Czp) is a well-known, electron-rich paracyclophane (PCP) molecule that allows to introduce planar chirality in organic emitters, notably those displaying thermally activated delayed fluorescence. In an attempt to optimize the preparation of Czp, an alternative synthetic route is explored, following a Suzuki–Cadogan sequence, affording a previously undescribed (3,9)-isomer. The shifted anchoring points give rise to clear structural and spectroscopic differences. The two ( 3,9)Czp enantiomers are successfully resolved and their chiroptical properties are determined in toluene solution. Considering the growing interest in π-conjugated polymers for circularly polarized optoelectronics, due to their ability to form chiral helical assemblies and thereby realize strong chiroptical activity, (3,9)Czp is then explored as a chiral small-molecule dopant for achiral poly(9,9-dioctylfluorene) (PFO). Polymer films containing 20 wt% of the S p -enantiomer display the strongest chiral amplification (following thermal treatment), reaching a g abs value of 3.0 × 10−2.
item.dc:creator: Louis Jackers, Rishi Shivhare, Giel Swennen, Gijs Verschaeve, Sonny Brebels, Tom Cardeynaels, Dmitry Levshov, Jan D’Haen, Kristof Van Hecke, Wouter Herrebout, Laurence Lutsen, Koen Vandewal, Wouter Maes
item.dc:title: (3,9)Carbazolophane – An Effective Chiral Dopant for Achiral Polyfluorene

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Iron–Carbene Activation Enables Stereocontrolled Glycosylations From Conventional Thioglycosides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A mild, neutral iron–carbene activation enables highly stereocontrolled glycosylations from conventional thioglycoside donors. The method tolerates diverse alcohols, including bioactive motifs, and is effective for glucose, galactose, mannose, and xylose. Iterative couplings streamline the assembly of well‐defined tetrasaccharides.
Stereocontrolled formation of glycosides remains one of the most persistent challenges in carbohydrate chemistry. Existing methods often require strongly acidic promoters, cryogenic temperatures, precious‐metal catalysts, or specially prefunctionalized donors. Here, we report a mild, neutral, and earth‐abundant iron–carbene‐mediated activation of readily accessible conventional thioglycoside donors that delivers 1,2‐cis and 1,2‐trans glycosides with excellent stereoselectivity. The access of 1,2‐cis selectivity is induced by utilizing conformationally locking protecting groups, and 1,2‐trans selectivity by neighboring group participation to direct the anomeric outcome and functions effectively with D‐glucose, D‐galactose, D‐mannose, D‐xylose, and acid‐sensitive functional group carrying glycosyl donors. A broad range of alcohol acceptors, including primary, secondary, tertiary, and bioactive motifs such as menthol, adamantanol, cholesterol, and oleanolic acid, is well tolerated. Furthermore, iterative coupling of furanosyl and pyranosyl thioglycosides enables streamlined assembly of a well‐defined, stereo‐enriched tetrasaccharide.
item.content:encoded:
A mild, neutral iron–carbene activation enables highly stereocontrolled glycosylations from conventional thioglycoside donors. The method tolerates diverse alcohols, including bioactive motifs, and is effective for glucose, galactose, mannose, and xylose. Iterative couplings streamline the assembly of well-defined tetrasaccharides.


Stereocontrolled formation of glycosides remains one of the most persistent challenges in carbohydrate chemistry. Existing methods often require strongly acidic promoters, cryogenic temperatures, precious-metal catalysts, or specially prefunctionalized donors. Here, we report a mild, neutral, and earth-abundant iron–carbene-mediated activation of readily accessible conventional thioglycoside donors that delivers 1,2-cis and 1,2-trans glycosides with excellent stereoselectivity. The access of 1,2-cis selectivity is induced by utilizing conformationally locking protecting groups, and 1,2-trans selectivity by neighboring group participation to direct the anomeric outcome and functions effectively with D-glucose, D-galactose, D-mannose, D-xylose, and acid-sensitive functional group carrying glycosyl donors. A broad range of alcohol acceptors, including primary, secondary, tertiary, and bioactive motifs such as menthol, adamantanol, cholesterol, and oleanolic acid, is well tolerated. Furthermore, iterative coupling of furanosyl and pyranosyl thioglycosides enables streamlined assembly of a well-defined, stereo-enriched tetrasaccharide.
item.dc:creator: Surya Pratap Singh, Shanthi Prasad Chinthamani, Indrajeet Sharma
item.dc:title: Iron–Carbene Activation Enables Stereocontrolled Glycosylations From Conventional Thioglycosides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrophotochemical CH Functionalization of Alkanes via Metal‐Catalyzed Hydrogen Atom Transfer
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review highlights C(sp3)H functionalization via synergistic metal‐photocatalyzed hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) and electrochemistry. Light excites a metal catalyst (W, Ce, Fe) to generate a HAT species, cleaving nonactivated C(sp3)H bonds. Applied potential drives catalyst turnover and pairs with diverse cathodic reactions (e.g., H2 evolution, Ni‐catalyzed coupling), enabling net‐oxidative or redox‐neutral transformations under mild conditions.
The direct and selective functionalization of unactivated C(sp3)H bonds under mild conditions represents a fundamental challenge in synthetic chemistry owing to their high bond dissociation energies and intrinsic nonpolarity. The integration of ligand‐to‐metal charge transfer (LMCT)‐enabled hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) with electrochemistry has emerged as a mild and sustainable strategy. This review provides a dedicated overview of recent advances in electrophotochemical C(sp3)H functionalization enabled by decatungstate, cerium‐based, and iron‐based catalytic systems, covering both net‐oxidative transformations coupled with cathodic hydrogen evolution and redox‐neutral processes through Fe/Ni‐based paired electrolysis. It details the mechanistic insights, highlights the unique advantages, and discusses current challenges and future directions for this promising approach to C(sp3)H bond valorization.
item.content:encoded:
This review highlights C(sp3)H functionalization via synergistic metal-photocatalyzed hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) and electrochemistry. Light excites a metal catalyst (W, Ce, Fe) to generate a HAT species, cleaving nonactivated C(sp3)H bonds. Applied potential drives catalyst turnover and pairs with diverse cathodic reactions (e.g., H2 evolution, Ni-catalyzed coupling), enabling net-oxidative or redox-neutral transformations under mild conditions.


The direct and selective functionalization of unactivated C(sp3)H bonds under mild conditions represents a fundamental challenge in synthetic chemistry owing to their high bond dissociation energies and intrinsic nonpolarity. The integration of ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT)-enabled hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) with electrochemistry has emerged as a mild and sustainable strategy. This review provides a dedicated overview of recent advances in electrophotochemical C(sp3)H functionalization enabled by decatungstate, cerium-based, and iron-based catalytic systems, covering both net-oxidative transformations coupled with cathodic hydrogen evolution and redox-neutral processes through Fe/Ni-based paired electrolysis. It details the mechanistic insights, highlights the unique advantages, and discusses current challenges and future directions for this promising approach to C(sp3)H bond valorization.
item.dc:creator: Qingyao Sun, Jifu Shi, Shiwei Zhang, Deqiang Liang
item.dc:title: Electrophotochemical CH Functionalization of Alkanes via Metal‐Catalyzed Hydrogen Atom Transfer

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Modular Synthesis of Isoindole/Indolizine Hybrids via the 1,3‐Dipolar Cycloaddition of Pyridine‐Based Mesoionics with Benzyne
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An approach to prepare pyrido[2,1‐a]isoindoles via the cycloaddition of pyridine‐based 1,3‐dipoles and benzyne is described, offering modular access to a range of substituted products by systematic tuning of the dipole.
A modular strategy for the synthesis of fused indolizine/isoindole hybrids, pyrido[2,1‐α]isoindoles, via cycloaddition of pyridine‐based mesoionic 1,3‐dipoles with benzyne is described. The method is effective across a broad range of substituted 1,3‐dipoles derived from pyridinyl acid chlorides and imines, enabling the incorporation of aromatic groups bearing electron‐donating or electron‐withdrawing substituents, extended conjugation, or aliphatic substituents onto the heteroaromatic framework from readily available and stable reagents.
item.content:encoded:
An approach to prepare pyrido[2,1-a]isoindoles via the cycloaddition of pyridine-based 1,3-dipoles and benzyne is described, offering modular access to a range of substituted products by systematic tuning of the dipole.


A modular strategy for the synthesis of fused indolizine/isoindole hybrids, pyrido[2,1-α]isoindoles, via cycloaddition of pyridine-based mesoionic 1,3-dipoles with benzyne is described. The method is effective across a broad range of substituted 1,3-dipoles derived from pyridinyl acid chlorides and imines, enabling the incorporation of aromatic groups bearing electron-donating or electron-withdrawing substituents, extended conjugation, or aliphatic substituents onto the heteroaromatic framework from readily available and stable reagents.
item.dc:creator: Samira Taghizadeh Nodehi, Mohammad Ghanbari, Bruce A. Arndtsen
item.dc:title: A Modular Synthesis of Isoindole/Indolizine Hybrids via the 1,3‐Dipolar Cycloaddition of Pyridine‐Based Mesoionics with Benzyne

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Hofmann Degradation of Asparagine and Glutamine as an Efficient Approach for the Synthesis of Derivatized Lysine Homologs
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
A practical and scalable route to C1 and C2 lysine homologs has been developed using Hofmann degradation of N‐protected L‐asparagine and L‐glutamine as the key step. These short‐tether scaffolds enable efficient synthesis of diverse Fmoc‐protected noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) bearing donor, acceptor, redox‐active, and fluorescent residues. The ncAAs were successfully incorporated into peptide hydrogelators by solid‐phase peptide synthesis.
We present a convenient and efficient approach for synthesizing lysine homologs via the Hofmann rearrangement of N‐protected L‐asparagine and L‐glutamine. This method enabled the preparation of a diverse family of noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) bearing side chains with varied properties, including electron‐donating and electron‐accepting abilities, redox activity, and fluorescence. The luminescence properties and redox behavior of these ncAAs were investigated. Furthermore, the newly synthesized Fmoc‐protected ncAAs were successfully incorporated into a hexapeptide sequence using solid‐phase peptide synthesis, thus expanding the library of hexapeptide hydrogelators.
item.content:encoded:
A practical and scalable route to C1 and C2 lysine homologs has been developed using Hofmann degradation of N-protected L-asparagine and L-glutamine as the key step. These short-tether scaffolds enable efficient synthesis of diverse Fmoc-protected noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) bearing donor, acceptor, redox-active, and fluorescent residues. The ncAAs were successfully incorporated into peptide hydrogelators by solid-phase peptide synthesis.


We present a convenient and efficient approach for synthesizing lysine homologs via the Hofmann rearrangement of N-protected L-asparagine and L-glutamine. This method enabled the preparation of a diverse family of noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) bearing side chains with varied properties, including electron-donating and electron-accepting abilities, redox activity, and fluorescence. The luminescence properties and redox behavior of these ncAAs were investigated. Furthermore, the newly synthesized Fmoc-protected ncAAs were successfully incorporated into a hexapeptide sequence using solid-phase peptide synthesis, thus expanding the library of hexapeptide hydrogelators.
item.dc:creator: Adelaide R. Mashweu, François J. De Beer, Jolien Bertouille, Steven Ballet, Charlotte Martin, Ulrich Hennecke, Charlene Marais, Vladimir A. Azov
item.dc:title: Hofmann Degradation of Asparagine and Glutamine as an Efficient Approach for the Synthesis of Derivatized Lysine Homologs

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesizing Multisubstituted Cyclohexanes via Palladium‐Catalyzed [4+2] Cycloaddition
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
Heavily substituted cyclohexanes are synthesized via palladium‐catalyzed [4+2] cycloaddition between methylene‐δ‐valerolactones and electron‐deficient trisubstituted olefins under mild reaction conditions, which provides an efficient method for the synthesis of multisubstituted cyclohexans from simple substrates.
Cyclohexanes bearing three contiguous stereocenters, two of which are all‐carbon quaternary centrers, are synthesized via palladium‐catalyzed [4+2] cycloaddition between methylene‐δ‐valerolactones and electron‐deficient trisubstituted olefins. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions and exhibits broad substrate scope. Preliminary studies using chiral ligands afforded the corresponding chiral adduct in good yield and moderate enantioselectivity.
item.content:encoded:
Heavily substituted cyclohexanes are synthesized via palladium-catalyzed [4+2] cycloaddition between methylene-δ-valerolactones and electron-deficient trisubstituted olefins under mild reaction conditions, which provides an efficient method for the synthesis of multisubstituted cyclohexans from simple substrates.


Cyclohexanes bearing three contiguous stereocenters, two of which are all-carbon quaternary centrers, are synthesized via palladium-catalyzed [4+2] cycloaddition between methylene-δ-valerolactones and electron-deficient trisubstituted olefins. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions and exhibits broad substrate scope. Preliminary studies using chiral ligands afforded the corresponding chiral adduct in good yield and moderate enantioselectivity.
item.dc:creator: Jiajun Mo, Xuyang Li, Jie Peng, Yungui Peng, Guihua Chen
item.dc:title: Synthesizing Multisubstituted Cyclohexanes via Palladium‐Catalyzed [4+2] Cycloaddition

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mild Oxidative Synthesis of N‐Formamides With Bio‐Based Pyruvaldehyde in Green Solvents
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
The application of pyruvaldehyde as a formyl equivalent for the N‐formylation of amines in ethanol under mild reaction conditions has been developed. This method features broad substrate scope, good functional group tolerance, and generate products in good yields.
This work represents a catalyst‐free green protocol for the N‐formylation of amines using pyruvaldehyde as a formyl source in ethanol under mild reaction conditions. This highly selective, catalyst‐free, and base‐free protocol enables the efficient synthesis of diverse formamides in moderate to excellent yields. It also offers a broad substrate scope, accommodating both structural and electronic variations of amines and amine‐containing heterocycles, thereby facilitating diversity generation. The avoidance of hazardous waste and the feasibility of gram‐scale synthesis represent prominent additional advantages.
item.content:encoded:
The application of pyruvaldehyde as a formyl equivalent for the N-formylation of amines in ethanol under mild reaction conditions has been developed. This method features broad substrate scope, good functional group tolerance, and generate products in good yields.


This work represents a catalyst-free green protocol for the N-formylation of amines using pyruvaldehyde as a formyl source in ethanol under mild reaction conditions. This highly selective, catalyst-free, and base-free protocol enables the efficient synthesis of diverse formamides in moderate to excellent yields. It also offers a broad substrate scope, accommodating both structural and electronic variations of amines and amine-containing heterocycles, thereby facilitating diversity generation. The avoidance of hazardous waste and the feasibility of gram-scale synthesis represent prominent additional advantages.

item.dc:title: Mild Oxidative Synthesis of N‐Formamides With Bio‐Based Pyruvaldehyde in Green Solvents

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Site‐Selective C–H/C–H Cross Dehydrogenative Coupling Between Quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐Ones and Aliphatic Nitriles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
An efficient and facile method was developed for the synthesis of C3‐cyanoalkylated quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐ones via a C–H/C–H cross dehydrogenation coupling between quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐ones and aliphatic nitriles.
An efficient and facile method was developed for the synthesis of C3‐cyanoalkylated quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐ones via a C–H/C–H cross dehydrogenation coupling between quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐ones and aliphatic nitriles. The method enables the β‐ or γ‐site‐selective C−H activation of aliphatic nitriles, giving the corresponding products in moderated to good yields. The reaction demonstrated a broad substrate scope and useful synthetic application ability.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient and facile method was developed for the synthesis of C3-cyanoalkylated quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones via a C–H/C–H cross dehydrogenation coupling between quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones and aliphatic nitriles.


An efficient and facile method was developed for the synthesis of C3-cyanoalkylated quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones via a C–H/C–H cross dehydrogenation coupling between quinoxalin-2(1H)-ones and aliphatic nitriles. The method enables the β- or γ-site-selective C−H activation of aliphatic nitriles, giving the corresponding products in moderated to good yields. The reaction demonstrated a broad substrate scope and useful synthetic application ability.
item.dc:creator: Yizhuo Xue, Yingjie Zhang, Chuan Li, Zeguo Fang, Qian Zhang, Dong Li
item.dc:title: Site‐Selective C–H/C–H Cross Dehydrogenative Coupling Between Quinoxalin‐2(1H)‐Ones and Aliphatic Nitriles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Radical Allylation and Benzylation of N‐Benzyl Ketimines with Allyl‐Bpins or 4‐Benzyl Hantzsch Esters Through Hydrogen Atom Transfer (HAT)/Photoredox Process
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
A photoinduced radical coupling of ketimines and nucleophilic alkyl‐Bpins or 4‐benzyl Hantzsch esters without the requirement of a strong base is presented.
In the past decade, the functionalization of N‐benzyl ketimines has emerged as an attractive way toward the concise synthesis of α‐substituted benzyl amine derivatives. Besides the traditional nucleophilic substitution with 2‐azaallyl anions to alkyl halides, recent progress has focused on CC bond formation via 2‐azaallyl radicals. These processes require the use of a strong base to convert N‐benzyl ketimines to 2‐azaallyl anions, which perform as organic super‐electron‐donors, and only electrophiles can be used as the radical precursors for the coupling partners. Herein, we present the photoinduced benzylation and allylation of N‐benzyl ketimines with nucleophiles as the radical precursors, such as allyl Bpins (pinacol boronic esters) and 4‐benzyl Hantzsch esters. Lauroyl peroxide is employed to mediate 2‐azaallyl radical generation via a hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) process and furnish the redox cycle of the photocatalyst.
item.content:encoded:
A photoinduced radical coupling of ketimines and nucleophilic alkyl-Bpins or 4-benzyl Hantzsch esters without the requirement of a strong base is presented.


In the past decade, the functionalization of N-benzyl ketimines has emerged as an attractive way toward the concise synthesis of α-substituted benzyl amine derivatives. Besides the traditional nucleophilic substitution with 2-azaallyl anions to alkyl halides, recent progress has focused on CC bond formation via 2-azaallyl radicals. These processes require the use of a strong base to convert N-benzyl ketimines to 2-azaallyl anions, which perform as organic super-electron-donors, and only electrophiles can be used as the radical precursors for the coupling partners. Herein, we present the photoinduced benzylation and allylation of N-benzyl ketimines with nucleophiles as the radical precursors, such as allyl Bpins (pinacol boronic esters) and 4-benzyl Hantzsch esters. Lauroyl peroxide is employed to mediate 2-azaallyl radical generation via a hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) process and furnish the redox cycle of the photocatalyst.
item.dc:creator: Jia Liu, Fengxuan Chu, Xule Shen, Zuo‐Qin Liang, Lan‐Gui Xie
item.dc:title: Radical Allylation and Benzylation of N‐Benzyl Ketimines with Allyl‐Bpins or 4‐Benzyl Hantzsch Esters Through Hydrogen Atom Transfer (HAT)/Photoredox Process

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Bisphosphonium Salt‐Catalyzed Facile Synthesis of Aldehydes and Ketones From Alkylarenes Under Air
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
A metal‐free visible‐light photocatalytic protocol has been established for the oxidation of alkylarenes to aromatic aldehydes and ketones, using bisphosphonium salt as a photoredox catalyst and ambient air as the sole oxidant under mild conditions (395 nm LED, room temperature, MeCN). This additive‐free approach exhibits good functional group tolerance across diverse substrates, affording moderate to excellent yields, while circumventing the use of pure O2 to enhance operational safety and practicality.
Herein, we report an efficient method for the oxidation of simple alkylarenes to the corresponding aldehydes or ketones under visible light irradiation, using molecular oxygen from ambient air as the oxidant and bisphosphonium salt as the photoredox catalyst. This protocol obviates the need for additional metal catalysts or additives, thus emerging as a facile and green alternative for the synthesis of such carbonyl compounds. Preliminary substrate exploration demonstrates that this method is compatible with a broad range of functionalized substrates.
item.content:encoded:
A metal-free visible-light photocatalytic protocol has been established for the oxidation of alkylarenes to aromatic aldehydes and ketones, using bisphosphonium salt as a photoredox catalyst and ambient air as the sole oxidant under mild conditions (395 nm LED, room temperature, MeCN). This additive-free approach exhibits good functional group tolerance across diverse substrates, affording moderate to excellent yields, while circumventing the use of pure O2 to enhance operational safety and practicality.


Herein, we report an efficient method for the oxidation of simple alkylarenes to the corresponding aldehydes or ketones under visible light irradiation, using molecular oxygen from ambient air as the oxidant and bisphosphonium salt as the photoredox catalyst. This protocol obviates the need for additional metal catalysts or additives, thus emerging as a facile and green alternative for the synthesis of such carbonyl compounds. Preliminary substrate exploration demonstrates that this method is compatible with a broad range of functionalized substrates.
item.dc:creator: Zhuo Li, Lili Wang, Zheng Duan
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Bisphosphonium Salt‐Catalyzed Facile Synthesis of Aldehydes and Ketones From Alkylarenes Under Air

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Ni‐Catalyzed Reductive Acyl Cross‐Coupling: Straightforward Synthesis of α‐Trifluoromethylthiolated Ketones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
The trifluoromethylthiol group (‐SCF3) has attracted considerable attention due to its high lipophilicity and strong electron‐withdrawing ability. Over the past decades, numerous methodologies have been developed for the construction of C(sp2)–SCF3 bonds through the selective introduction of the SCF3 group into arenes or vinyl derivatives. In contrast, the synthesis of tertiary C(sp3)‐SCF3 compounds remains more restricted. Herein, we present a Ni‐catalyzed reductive acyl cross‐coupling that enables the direct construction of α‐trifluoromethylthiolated ketones in a single step utilizing a recently developed SCF3‐containing electrophiles. Moreover, mechanistic investigations have demonstrated the presence of an unusual pathway for reductive coupling and the essential role of zinc.

Over the past decades, numerous methodologies have been developed for the construction of C(sp2)–SCF3 bonds through the selective introduction of the SCF3 group into arenes or vinyl derivatives. In contrast, the synthesis of tertiary C(sp3)‐SCF3 compounds remains more restricted. Herein, we present a Ni‐catalyzed reductive acyl cross‐coupling that enables the direct construction of α‐trifluoromethylthiolated ketones in a single step utilizing a recently developed SCF3‐containing electrophiles. Moreover, mechanistic investigations have demonstrated the presence of an unusual pathway for reductive coupling and the essential role of zinc.

Over the past decades, numerous methodologies have been developed for the construction of C(sp2)–SCF3 bonds through the selective introduction of the SCF3 group into arenes or vinyl derivatives. In contrast, the synthesis of tertiary C(sp3)-SCF3 compounds remains more restricted. Herein, we present a Ni-catalyzed reductive acyl cross-coupling that enables the direct construction of α-trifluoromethylthiolated ketones in a single step utilizing a recently developed SCF3-containing electrophiles. Moreover, mechanistic investigations have demonstrated the presence of an unusual pathway for reductive coupling and the essential role of zinc.

Herein, we present a Ni-catalyzed reductive acyl cross-coupling that enables the direct construction of α-trifluoromethylthiolated ketones in a single step utilizing a recently developed SCF3-containing electrophiles. Moreover, mechanistic investigations have demonstrated the presence of an unusual pathway for reductive coupling and the essential role of zinc.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photocatalytic Synthesis of Imidazoline and Oxazolidine Derivatives Using Organic Catalysts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we report a synthetic strategy that relies on a self‐developed organic photocatalyst for the efficient construction of imidazolines and oxazolidines. Using readily available 4‐methyl‐N‐(2 phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox‐active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox‐initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate‐type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.
Herein, we report a synthetic strategy that relies on a self‐developed organic photocatalyst for the efficient construction of imidazolines and oxazolidines. Using readily available 4‐methyl‐N‐(2‐phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox‐active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox‐initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate‐type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

Using readily available 4-methyl-N-(2-phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox-active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox-initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate-type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis and Physicochemical Properties of trans‐2‐Fluoroalkyl‐Substituted Cyclopentane Building Blocks
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
A scalable approach to the multigram synthesis of trans−1,2‐fluoroalkyl‐substituted cyclopentane building blocks based on orthogonal functionalization of a common precursor is described. The effect of the fluoroalkyl substituents on the compound's physicochemical properties (pKa and LogP) was established for a series of model derivatives.
A scalable synthesis of trans−1,2‐fluoroalkyl‐substituted cyclopentane building blocks was developed. The approach relies on orthogonal late‐stage functionalization of a common precursor – monoester of trans−1,2‐cyclopentanecarboxylic acid. Fluoromethyl‐substituted derivatives were accessed via exhaustive reduction of the COOH group, activation of the resulting primary alcohol as a triflate, and nucleophilic substitution with fluoride. Meanwhile, difluoro‐ and trifluoromethyl groups were introduced through deoxofluorination of the corresponding aldehyde or carboxylic acid. The resulting fluorinated esters were efficiently diversified into a broad range of building blocks relevant to medicinal chemistry, including carboxylic acids, primary amines, azides, alcohols, and sulfonyl chlorides. In addition to the synthetic developments, the effect of the fluoroalkyl substituent (CH2F, CHF2, CF3) on physicochemical properties of the cyclopentane derivatives was established by measuring pKa and LogP values of model derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
A scalable approach to the multigram synthesis of trans−1,2-fluoroalkyl-substituted cyclopentane building blocks based on orthogonal functionalization of a common precursor is described. The effect of the fluoroalkyl substituents on the compound's physicochemical properties (pK a and LogP) was established for a series of model derivatives.


A scalable synthesis of trans−1,2-fluoroalkyl-substituted cyclopentane building blocks was developed. The approach relies on orthogonal late-stage functionalization of a common precursor – monoester of trans−1,2-cyclopentanecarboxylic acid. Fluoromethyl-substituted derivatives were accessed via exhaustive reduction of the COOH group, activation of the resulting primary alcohol as a triflate, and nucleophilic substitution with fluoride. Meanwhile, difluoro- and trifluoromethyl groups were introduced through deoxofluorination of the corresponding aldehyde or carboxylic acid. The resulting fluorinated esters were efficiently diversified into a broad range of building blocks relevant to medicinal chemistry, including carboxylic acids, primary amines, azides, alcohols, and sulfonyl chlorides. In addition to the synthetic developments, the effect of the fluoroalkyl substituent (CH2F, CHF2, CF3) on physicochemical properties of the cyclopentane derivatives was established by measuring pK a and LogP values of model derivatives.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Front Cover: Hofmann Degradation of Asparagine and Glutamine as an Efficient Approach for the Synthesis of Derivatized Lysine Homologs (Eur. J. Org. Chem. 21/2026)
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description: The Front Cover shows how a concise Hofmann‐rearrangement route from protected asparagine and glutamine provides short‐chain C1/C2 lysine homologs as versatile platforms for noncanonical amino acid synthesis. Donor, acceptor, fluorescent, and redox‐active residues were introduced into peptide hydrogelators by solid‐phase peptide synthesis, thereby enabling the synthesis of functional biomaterials with tunable self‐assembly, fluorescence, and redox‐responsive behavior. More information can be found in the Research Article by V. A. Azov and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/ejoc.70413).
item.content:encoded: The Front Cover shows how a concise Hofmann-rearrangement route from protected asparagine and glutamine provides short-chain C1/C2 lysine homologs as versatile platforms for noncanonical amino acid synthesis. Donor, acceptor, fluorescent, and redox-active residues were introduced into peptide hydrogelators by solid-phase peptide synthesis, thereby enabling the synthesis of functional biomaterials with tunable self-assembly, fluorescence, and redox-responsive behavior. More information can be found in the Research Article by V. A. Azov and co-workers (DOI: 10.1002/ejoc.70413).

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: HFIP‐Mediated Electrophilic Chemistry: Synthetic Utility and Mechanistic Insights
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
HFIP: A remarkable multifunctional solvent for Electrophilic chemistry and for stabilization of cationic intermediates.
Hexafluoroisopropanol has emerged as a distinctive solvent, recognized for its ability to facilitate a variety of difficult chemical transformations. Its application has extended beyond conventional roles, serving effectively as a solvent, cosolvent, additive, and acidic promoter in organic synthesis. The compound's exceptional characteristics, particularly its high polarity and minimal nucleophilicity, enable the stabilization of cationic intermediates from a variety of sources, making it especially valuable in reactions involving electrophilic species.
item.content:encoded:
HFIP: A remarkable multifunctional solvent for Electrophilic chemistry and for stabilization of cationic intermediates.


Hexafluoroisopropanol has emerged as a distinctive solvent, recognized for its ability to facilitate a variety of difficult chemical transformations. Its application has extended beyond conventional roles, serving effectively as a solvent, cosolvent, additive, and acidic promoter in organic synthesis. The compound's exceptional characteristics, particularly its high polarity and minimal nucleophilicity, enable the stabilization of cationic intermediates from a variety of sources, making it especially valuable in reactions involving electrophilic species.

item.dc:title: HFIP‐Mediated Electrophilic Chemistry: Synthetic Utility and Mechanistic Insights

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Emerging Trends in Organic Photoreactions Utilizing Disulfides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
Disulfides are versatile, nontoxic alternatives to metal photocatalysts. This review explores their role as photoactivated thiyl‐radical precursors and bifunctional catalysts (HAT/electron shuttling) in diverse transformations. Strategies for electronic tuning and development into recyclable heterogeneous materials are highlighted for sustainable, green photochemistry.
The facile photolysis of the sulfur‐sulfur bond in disulfides has emerged as a powerful and versatile strategy in organic chemistry. This review surveys the rapidly growing field of disulfide‐mediated photoreactions, including both catalytic and cocatalytic systems, as well as their roles across homogeneous and developing heterogeneous platforms. We highlight how electronically tuned disulfides function as photoactivated thiyl‐radical precursors and, in many cases, as bifunctional radical mediators that enable hydrogen‐atom transfer (HAT), electron shuttling, and polarity‐matched radical trapping. Representative reaction classes include anti‐Markovnikov hydrofunctionalizations of alkenes, oxidative alkene oxygenation and CC cleavage, hydrophosphinylation, hydrofluoroalkylation, decarboxylative transformations, C(sp3)–H functionalizations, and alkene/alkyne isomerizations. The review discusses the strategies for translating molecular disulfide chemistry into recyclable heterogeneous materials and polymer‐supported catalysts. The photochemical flexibility and tunability of disulfides through electronic modification, combined with their commercial availability and low toxicity, position them as attractive alternatives to expensive precious‐metal photoredox catalysts for advancing industrially viable and sustainable photochemistry.
item.content:encoded:
Disulfides are versatile, nontoxic alternatives to metal photocatalysts. This review explores their role as photoactivated thiyl-radical precursors and bifunctional catalysts (HAT/electron shuttling) in diverse transformations. Strategies for electronic tuning and development into recyclable heterogeneous materials are highlighted for sustainable, green photochemistry.


The facile photolysis of the sulfur-sulfur bond in disulfides has emerged as a powerful and versatile strategy in organic chemistry. This review surveys the rapidly growing field of disulfide-mediated photoreactions, including both catalytic and cocatalytic systems, as well as their roles across homogeneous and developing heterogeneous platforms. We highlight how electronically tuned disulfides function as photoactivated thiyl-radical precursors and, in many cases, as bifunctional radical mediators that enable hydrogen-atom transfer (HAT), electron shuttling, and polarity-matched radical trapping. Representative reaction classes include anti-Markovnikov hydrofunctionalizations of alkenes, oxidative alkene oxygenation and CC cleavage, hydrophosphinylation, hydrofluoroalkylation, decarboxylative transformations, C(sp3)–H functionalizations, and alkene/alkyne isomerizations. The review discusses the strategies for translating molecular disulfide chemistry into recyclable heterogeneous materials and polymer-supported catalysts. The photochemical flexibility and tunability of disulfides through electronic modification, combined with their commercial availability and low toxicity, position them as attractive alternatives to expensive precious-metal photoredox catalysts for advancing industrially viable and sustainable photochemistry.
item.dc:creator: Sunil Kumar, Bolormaa Bayarkhuu, Hong Y. Cho, Jeehye Byun
item.dc:title: Emerging Trends in Organic Photoreactions Utilizing Disulfides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Gold‐Catalyzed Photoannulation: Access to Cyclic Scaffolds
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
Merging of gold catalysis with photochemical activation has introduced synthetically valuable reactivity pattern and opened new avenues for the design of cyclic architectures. The present review shades light on recent advancements in gold‐enabled photocyclizations to build up diverse carbo‐ and heterocyclic scaffolds.
Gold catalysis has emerged as a revolutionary field in organic synthetic chemistry. The combination of gold catalysis with photochemical activation has further expanded this area, introducing synthetically valuable modes of reactivity. In fact, gold photocatalysis eliminates the need for stoichiometric sacrificial external oxidants and unlocks opportunitiesfor broader range of radical‐mediated transformations. In recent years, significant progress has been achieved in the development of gold‐catalyzed cyclization reactions facilitated by visible‐light irradiation. The present review highlights last 5 year advancements in gold‐enabled photocyclizations to build up diverse carbo‐ and heterocyclic scaffolds. This review offers comprehensive insights into mechanistic aspects and their significance in contemporary synthetic chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
Merging of gold catalysis with photochemical activation has introduced synthetically valuable reactivity pattern and opened new avenues for the design of cyclic architectures. The present review shades light on recent advancements in gold-enabled photocyclizations to build up diverse carbo- and heterocyclic scaffolds.


Gold catalysis has emerged as a revolutionary field in organic synthetic chemistry. The combination of gold catalysis with photochemical activation has further expanded this area, introducing synthetically valuable modes of reactivity. In fact, gold photocatalysis eliminates the need for stoichiometric sacrificial external oxidants and unlocks opportunitiesfor broader range of radical-mediated transformations. In recent years, significant progress has been achieved in the development of gold-catalyzed cyclization reactions facilitated by visible-light irradiation. The present review highlights last 5 year advancements in gold-enabled photocyclizations to build up diverse carbo- and heterocyclic scaffolds. This review offers comprehensive insights into mechanistic aspects and their significance in contemporary synthetic chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Suven Das
item.dc:title: Gold‐Catalyzed Photoannulation: Access to Cyclic Scaffolds

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chemical Syntheses of 3′ and 5′ Phosphorylated Oligonucleotides: An Overview
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 21, 4 June 2026.
item.dc:description:
This review provides an overview of the various chemical methods employed in the synthesis of oligonucleotides bearing a phosphate group at their 3′ or 5′ end. Notably, it presents modified phosphoramidites and supports developed for this purpose.
Oligonucleotides (ON) with a phosphate monoester group at their 3′ or 5′ end have applications in many fields. Initially, the reagents used for their synthesis were similar to those used for the introduction of internucleotidic phosphates. However, as nucleic acid chemistry evolved, demanding specifications (RNA compatibility, purification aid, simplicity of the deprotection reaction) led to the development of more and more sophisticated reagents. This review will present the evolution and the most recent methods of 3′ or 5′ terminal phosphorylation of oligonucleotides. It will also recall the criteria to be met in the quest for the ideal reagent.
item.content:encoded:
This review provides an overview of the various chemical methods employed in the synthesis of oligonucleotides bearing a phosphate group at their 3′ or 5′ end. Notably, it presents modified phosphoramidites and supports developed for this purpose.

Initially, the reagents used for their synthesis were similar to those used for the introduction of internucleotidic phosphates. However, as nucleic acid chemistry evolved, demanding specifications (RNA compatibility, purification aid, simplicity of the deprotection reaction) led to the development of more and more sophisticated reagents. This review will present the evolution and the most recent methods of 3′ or 5′ terminal phosphorylation of oligonucleotides. It will also recall the criteria to be met in the quest for the ideal reagent.
