item.dc:title: Chemical Syntheses of 3′ and 5′ Phosphorylated Oligonucleotides: An Overview

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Graphitic Carbon Nitride Enabled Photocatalytic Synthesis of 3‐Hydroxyisoindolin‐1‐ones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A sustainable, metal‐free photocatalytic protocol using graphitic carbon nitride (g‐C3N4) enables the benzylation of phthalimide derivatives from aryl acetic acids via radical–radical coupling, delivering high yields, broad functional group tolerance, catalyst recyclability, and scalability.
We report a sustainable and metal free approach for the benzylation of phthalimide derivatives using readily available (hetero)aryl acetic acids via a decarboxylative, visible‐light‐induced photooxidation process catalyzed by graphitic carbon nitride (g‐C3N4) and afforded the desired benzylated products in excellent yields within a reaction time of 5–6 h. The methodology features a broad substrate scope and high functional group tolerance, accommodating diverse (hetero)aryl and phthalimide derivatives. Importantly, this protocol avoids the use of transition metals and harsh oxidants, operates under mild conditions, and benefits from the recyclability of the heterogeneous photocatalyst, making it an efficient and environmentally benign strategy for CC bond formation.
item.content:encoded:
A sustainable, metal-free photocatalytic protocol using graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) enables the benzylation of phthalimide derivatives from aryl acetic acids via radical–radical coupling, delivering high yields, broad functional group tolerance, catalyst recyclability, and scalability.


We report a sustainable and metal free approach for the benzylation of phthalimide derivatives using readily available (hetero)aryl acetic acids via a decarboxylative, visible-light-induced photooxidation process catalyzed by graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and afforded the desired benzylated products in excellent yields within a reaction time of 5–6 h. The methodology features a broad substrate scope and high functional group tolerance, accommodating diverse (hetero)aryl and phthalimide derivatives. Importantly, this protocol avoids the use of transition metals and harsh oxidants, operates under mild conditions, and benefits from the recyclability of the heterogeneous photocatalyst, making it an efficient and environmentally benign strategy for CC bond formation.
item.dc:creator: Sangita Bishi, Gaetano Pastore, Peter Kreitmeier, Oliver Reiser, Debayan Sarkar
item.dc:title: Graphitic Carbon Nitride Enabled Photocatalytic Synthesis of 3‐Hydroxyisoindolin‐1‐ones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mesityl‐Substituted Azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3‐b]quinoxalines: Synthesis, Functionalization, Photophysical, and Electrochemical Properties
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3‐b]quinoxaline with a mesityl group as a solubilizing group was synthesized. By synthesizing derivatives in which various functional groups were introduced into two electronically distinct sites, the pyrrole nitrogen atom and the azulene five‐membered ring, we were able to reveal that the photophysical and electrochemical properties are significantly affected by the frontier orbital character of the substitution site.
Azulene‐fused heterocycles have attracted attention because they induce an effective donor–acceptor interaction, leading to a smaller highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)–lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) gap that exhibits characteristic photophysical and electronic properties. The synthesis of mesitylene‐substituted azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3‐b]quinoxaline was established in high yield by the condensation of azulene‐analog of isatin derivative with 1,2‐phenylenediamine. Functionalization of the azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3‐b]quinoxaline framework was successfully accomplished by N‐substitution at the pyrrole N‐atom and electrophilic substitution at the five‐membered ring site of the azulene moiety. Introduction of electron‐withdrawing tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD) units at both the pyrrole N‐atom and the azulene ring was accomplished via formal [2 + 2] cycloaddition–retroelectrocyclization (CA–RE) with tetracyanoethylene. The electronic characteristics of the resulting azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3‐b]quinoxalines were elucidated by spectroscopic and voltammetric analyses, supported by density functional theory calculations.
item.content:encoded:
Azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3-b]quinoxaline with a mesityl group as a solubilizing group was synthesized. By synthesizing derivatives in which various functional groups were introduced into two electronically distinct sites, the pyrrole nitrogen atom and the azulene five-membered ring, we were able to reveal that the photophysical and electrochemical properties are significantly affected by the frontier orbital character of the substitution site.


Azulene-fused heterocycles have attracted attention because they induce an effective donor–acceptor interaction, leading to a smaller highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)–lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) gap that exhibits characteristic photophysical and electronic properties. The synthesis of mesitylene-substituted azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3-b]quinoxaline was established in high yield by the condensation of azulene-analog of isatin derivative with 1,2-phenylenediamine. Functionalization of the azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3-b]quinoxaline framework was successfully accomplished by N-substitution at the pyrrole N-atom and electrophilic substitution at the five-membered ring site of the azulene moiety. Introduction of electron-withdrawing tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD) units at both the pyrrole N-atom and the azulene ring was accomplished via formal [2 + 2] cycloaddition–retroelectrocyclization (CA–RE) with tetracyanoethylene. The electronic characteristics of the resulting azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3-b]quinoxalines were elucidated by spectroscopic and voltammetric analyses, supported by density functional theory calculations.
item.dc:creator: Ryuta Sekiguchi, Hiroki Mizuno, Ikumi Uchiyama, Souta Yuruka, Atsuya Shimizu, Taku Shoji, Shunji Ito
item.dc:title: Mesityl‐Substituted Azuleno[1′,2′:4,5]pyrrolo[2,3‐b]quinoxalines: Synthesis, Functionalization, Photophysical, and Electrochemical Properties

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Collective Total Synthesis of Five Styryllactones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We have successfully developed an efficient approach for the rapid construction of diversely functionalized styryllactone frameworks. Taken together with a sequential reaction of phenyllithium mediated Bernet–Vasella‐type elimination/nucleophilic addition by a one‐pot process and a cascade reaction of olefin cross metathesis/butanediacetal deprotection/intramolecular oxa‐Michael addition/lactonization, this approach has facilitated the divergent total syntheses of five styryllactones (‐)‐goniofufurone, (‐) goniopypyrone, (‐)‐7‐epi‐goniofufurone, (‐)‐crassalactone B, and (‐)‐crassalactone C in five or six longest linear steps by divergent strategies.
The stereoselective syntheses of five styryllactones (−)‐goniofufurone, (−)‐goniopypyrone, (−)‐7‐epi‐goniofufurone, (−)‐crassalactone B, and (−)‐crassalactone C have been achieved in five or six longest linear steps by divergent strategies starting from chiral pool methyl α‐D‐glucopyranoside, respectively. The key transformations include a sequential reaction of Bernet–Vasella‐type reductive elimination and nucleophilic addition in a one‐pot process and a cascade reaction of cross‐metathesis/deprotection/intramolecular oxa‐Michael addition/lactonization involving the rapid construction of the challenging dioxabicyclo[3.3.0]octane and dioxabicyclo[3.3.1]nonan‐3‐one scaffolds.
item.content:encoded:
We have successfully developed an efficient approach for the rapid construction of diversely functionalized styryllactone frameworks. Taken together with a sequential reaction of phenyllithium mediated Bernet–Vasella-type elimination/nucleophilic addition by a one-pot process and a cascade reaction of olefin cross metathesis/butanediacetal deprotection/intramolecular oxa-Michael addition/lactonization, this approach has facilitated the divergent total syntheses of five styryllactones (-)-goniofufurone, (-) goniopypyrone, (-)-7-epi-goniofufurone, (-)-crassalactone B, and (-)-crassalactone C in five or six longest linear steps by divergent strategies.


The stereoselective syntheses of five styryllactones (−)-goniofufurone, (−)-goniopypyrone, (−)-7-epi-goniofufurone, (−)-crassalactone B, and (−)-crassalactone C have been achieved in five or six longest linear steps by divergent strategies starting from chiral pool methyl α-D-glucopyranoside, respectively. The key transformations include a sequential reaction of Bernet–Vasella-type reductive elimination and nucleophilic addition in a one-pot process and a cascade reaction of cross-metathesis/deprotection/intramolecular oxa-Michael addition/lactonization involving the rapid construction of the challenging dioxabicyclo[3.3.0]octane and dioxabicyclo[3.3.1]nonan-3-one scaffolds.
item.dc:creator: Yifei Zhang, Linjin Li, Pengpeng Nie, Wei Duan, Da Zhao, Jun Liu, Yuguo Du
item.dc:title: Collective Total Synthesis of Five Styryllactones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Cu(OTf)2‐Promoted Synthesis of Pyrimidine‐Fused Quinolines Using Rongalite as a C1 Synthon
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Here, we propose a Cu(OTf)2‐promoted [3 + 2 + 1] cycloaddition strategy for the efficient synthesis of pyrimidine‐fused quinolines using rongalite as a readily available and low‐cost C1 synthon. This protocol is suitable for large‐scale synthesis and downstream derivatization. Moreover, the functionalized derivatives exhibit typical aggregation‐induced emission characteristics and viscosity‐responsive fluorescence behavior, highlighting their potential photophysical applications.
A Cu(OTf)2‐promoted one‐pot three‐component reaction using rongalite as a C1 synthon for the synthesis of pyrimidine‐fused quinoline compounds has been reported. This protocol is characterized by readily available chemicals and straightforward operation, delivering the products in moderate to good yields (32 examples, 21%–86% yields). This strategy is suitable for large‐scale preparation and late‐stage derivatizations. The product with simple modifications exhibits significant aggregation‐induced emission properties and viscosity‐responsive fluorescence behavior.
item.content:encoded:
Here, we propose a Cu(OTf)2-promoted [3 + 2 + 1] cycloaddition strategy for the efficient synthesis of pyrimidine-fused quinolines using rongalite as a readily available and low-cost C1 synthon. This protocol is suitable for large-scale synthesis and downstream derivatization. Moreover, the functionalized derivatives exhibit typical aggregation-induced emission characteristics and viscosity-responsive fluorescence behavior, highlighting their potential photophysical applications.


A Cu(OTf)2-promoted one-pot three-component reaction using rongalite as a C1 synthon for the synthesis of pyrimidine-fused quinoline compounds has been reported. This protocol is characterized by readily available chemicals and straightforward operation, delivering the products in moderate to good yields (32 examples, 21%–86% yields). This strategy is suitable for large-scale preparation and late-stage derivatizations. The product with simple modifications exhibits significant aggregation-induced emission properties and viscosity-responsive fluorescence behavior.
item.dc:creator: Xueyan Lv, Zhiming Xu, Zongrui Hu, Qingbo Jia, Caiwei Yang, Chen Ma
item.dc:title: Cu(OTf)2‐Promoted Synthesis of Pyrimidine‐Fused Quinolines Using Rongalite as a C1 Synthon

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Properties of [Fe(salen)]2‐μ‐Oxo Derivatives and Their Effect on Catalytic Hydrophosphination
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A series of [Fe(salen)]2‐μ‐oxo and [Fe(salophen)]2‐μ‐oxo complexes were synthesized to probe how ligand electronics and backbone rigidity influence redox behavior and catalytic hydrophosphination of styrene. Electron‐withdrawing substituents enhance stability and activity, enabling tunable, efficient C–P bond formation.
A series of [Fe(salen)]2(μ‐O) and [Fe(salophen)]2(μ‐O) complexes (1a to 1i) were synthesized and characterized to investigate the effect of ligand electronics and backbone structure on their physical and catalytic properties. UV‐visible spectroscopy revealed that complexes bearing aromatic salophen ligands exhibited minimal variation in the ligand‐to‐metal charge transfer (LMCT) band, whereas electron withdrawing and donating groups on the phenoxy fragment of salen (1a to 1c) showed significant shifts, indicating tunable electronic properties. Cyclic voltammetry confirmed reversible Fe(III)/(II) redox couples for the salen complexes with formal potentials ranging from −0.95 to −1.42 V (vs. Fc/Fc+), demonstrating the sensitivity of the iron center to phenolate substituents and backbone length. Infrared spectroscopy of the Fe–O–Fe stretch correlated electronic effects with bond strength. Catalytic hydrophosphination of styrene with diphenylphosphine established 1a to 1c as the most active precatalysts, affording up to 81% yield of the tertiary phosphine product. In situ IR and NMR studies indicated rapid Fe–O–Fe bond cleavage to generate mononuclear Fe(III) species. A postulated mechanism involving an active iron phosphide intermediate (3a) and an inactive iron hydroxide by‐product (4a) is presented.
item.content:encoded:
A series of [Fe(salen)]2-μ-oxo and [Fe(salophen)]2-μ-oxo complexes were synthesized to probe how ligand electronics and backbone rigidity influence redox behavior and catalytic hydrophosphination of styrene. Electron-withdrawing substituents enhance stability and activity, enabling tunable, efficient C–P bond formation.


A series of [Fe(salen)]2(μ-O) and [Fe(salophen)]2(μ-O) complexes (1a to 1i) were synthesized and characterized to investigate the effect of ligand electronics and backbone structure on their physical and catalytic properties. UV-visible spectroscopy revealed that complexes bearing aromatic salophen ligands exhibited minimal variation in the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) band, whereas electron withdrawing and donating groups on the phenoxy fragment of salen (1a to 1c) showed significant shifts, indicating tunable electronic properties. Cyclic voltammetry confirmed reversible Fe(III)/(II) redox couples for the salen complexes with formal potentials ranging from −0.95 to −1.42 V (vs. Fc/Fc+), demonstrating the sensitivity of the iron center to phenolate substituents and backbone length. Infrared spectroscopy of the Fe–O–Fe stretch correlated electronic effects with bond strength. Catalytic hydrophosphination of styrene with diphenylphosphine established 1a to 1c as the most active precatalysts, affording up to 81% yield of the tertiary phosphine product. In situ IR and NMR studies indicated rapid Fe–O–Fe bond cleavage to generate mononuclear Fe(III) species. A postulated mechanism involving an active iron phosphide intermediate (3a) and an inactive iron hydroxide by-product (4a) is presented.
item.dc:creator: Nathan J. Buxton, Edmund O. Dadge, Oliver S. Matys, Max A. A. Entwisle, Ruth L. Webster
item.dc:title: Properties of [Fe(salen)]2‐μ‐Oxo Derivatives and Their Effect on Catalytic Hydrophosphination

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Cyclic Sulfamates Through [5 + 1] Cyclization of Sulfuryl Fluoride and Salicylic Amides: Synthesis and Functionalization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An efficient route to benzo[e][1,2,3]oxathiazine‐4(3H)‐one 2,2‐dioxides is reported via a simple one‐pot base‐promoted [5 + 1] cyclization of salicylic amides and sulfuryl fluoride. The resulting cyclic sulfamates can be readily diversified (e.g., through deoxochlorination, amination, and Pd‐catalyzed couplings) to access lead‐like chemical space.
An efficient sulfuryl fluoride‐enabled synthesis of benzo[e][1,2,3]oxathiazine‐4(3H)‐one‐2,2‐dioxides from salicylic amides is described. Salicylamides were prepared on a multigram scale, and the corresponding cyclic sulfamates were synthesized via operationally simple one‐pot Et3N‐promoted [5 + 1] cyclization. In some cases, intermediate products of O‐sulfonylation (fluorosulfates) were isolated. The method was suitable for both primary and secondary salicylic amides, albeit the use of a stronger base was necessary for sterically hindered substrates. The synthetic utility of the obtained heterocycles was demonstrated by their further chemical transformations. In particular, 4‐chloro derivatives were obtained by deoxochlorination and converted into various cyclic aminosulfamates on a multigram scale. Derivatives bearing a bromine atom in the benzene ring were subjected to Pd‐catalyzed couplings without affecting the sulfamate ring. Physicochemical properties of a representative virtual library derived from the synthesized scaffolds met the criteria of lead‐likeness guidelines.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient route to benzo[e][1,2,3]oxathiazine-4(3H)-one 2,2-dioxides is reported via a simple one-pot base-promoted [5 + 1] cyclization of salicylic amides and sulfuryl fluoride. The resulting cyclic sulfamates can be readily diversified (e.g., through deoxochlorination, amination, and Pd-catalyzed couplings) to access lead-like chemical space.


An efficient sulfuryl fluoride-enabled synthesis of benzo[e][1,2,3]oxathiazine-4(3H)-one-2,2-dioxides from salicylic amides is described. Salicylamides were prepared on a multigram scale, and the corresponding cyclic sulfamates were synthesized via operationally simple one-pot Et3N-promoted [5 + 1] cyclization. In some cases, intermediate products of O-sulfonylation (fluorosulfates) were isolated. The method was suitable for both primary and secondary salicylic amides, albeit the use of a stronger base was necessary for sterically hindered substrates. The synthetic utility of the obtained heterocycles was demonstrated by their further chemical transformations. In particular, 4-chloro derivatives were obtained by deoxochlorination and converted into various cyclic aminosulfamates on a multigram scale. Derivatives bearing a bromine atom in the benzene ring were subjected to Pd-catalyzed couplings without affecting the sulfamate ring. Physicochemical properties of a representative virtual library derived from the synthesized scaffolds met the criteria of lead-likeness guidelines.
item.dc:creator: Viktoriia S. Sidorska, Serhii H. Aleksandrenko, Illia O. Doroshenko, Bohdan V. Vashchenko, Serhii Zhersh, Oleksandr O. Grygorenko
item.dc:title: Cyclic Sulfamates Through [5 + 1] Cyclization of Sulfuryl Fluoride and Salicylic Amides: Synthesis and Functionalization

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoredox/Palladium Promoted Selective Dialkylation of α,α′‐Amino C(sp3)H Bonds from N,N‐Dimethyl‐1‐sulfonyl Enamines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We have established a facile and operationally simple protocol for the direct dialkylation of N,N‐dimethyl‐1‐sulfonyl enamines employing a dual photoredox and palladium catalytic system. The method excels in its compatibility with alkenyl retention, enabling the synthesis of structurally diverse, electron‐deficient 1‐sulfonyl‐2‐N,N‐dialkyl enamines with high functional group tolerance and perfect regioselectivity. The process is characterized by a stepwise dialkylation mechanism involving electronically neutral photoredox catalysis, with the second alkylation step highlighting the pivotal role of palladium catalyst.
Enamines serve as significant building blocks in α‐(asymmetric) functionalization of aldehydes and key intermediates in the direct deoxygenation of amides to β‐functionalized amines. However, examples on the selective alkylation of α‐amino C(sp3)H bonds of enamines are rare, and especially, the dialkylation of α,α′‐amino C(sp3)H bonds is still less explored. Herein, we report an acridinium–palladium‐catalyzed operationally simple method for the facile dialkylation of N,N‐dimethyl‐1‐sulfonyl enamines, which is particularly efficient for the alkenyl retention cases, providing diverse electron‐deficient N,N‐dialkyl‐1‐sulfonyl enamines with exceptional functional group tolerance and complete regiocontrol. Control experiments and detailed mechanism study reveal that the electron neutral photoredox catalysis proceeds through a step‐wise mechanism, and the radical palladium intermediate plays an important role in the second alkylation progress.
item.content:encoded:
We have established a facile and operationally simple protocol for the direct dialkylation of N,N-dimethyl-1-sulfonyl enamines employing a dual photoredox and palladium catalytic system. The method excels in its compatibility with alkenyl retention, enabling the synthesis of structurally diverse, electron-deficient 1-sulfonyl-2-N,N-dialkyl enamines with high functional group tolerance and perfect regioselectivity. The process is characterized by a stepwise dialkylation mechanism involving electronically neutral photoredox catalysis, with the second alkylation step highlighting the pivotal role of palladium catalyst.


Enamines serve as significant building blocks in α-(asymmetric) functionalization of aldehydes and key intermediates in the direct deoxygenation of amides to β-functionalized amines. However, examples on the selective alkylation of α-amino C(sp3)H bonds of enamines are rare, and especially, the dialkylation of α,α′-amino C(sp3)H bonds is still less explored. Herein, we report an acridinium–palladium-catalyzed operationally simple method for the facile dialkylation of N,N-dimethyl-1-sulfonyl enamines, which is particularly efficient for the alkenyl retention cases, providing diverse electron-deficient N,N-dialkyl-1-sulfonyl enamines with exceptional functional group tolerance and complete regiocontrol. Control experiments and detailed mechanism study reveal that the electron neutral photoredox catalysis proceeds through a step-wise mechanism, and the radical palladium intermediate plays an important role in the second alkylation progress.
item.dc:creator: Ankaier Tuersuntuoheti, Yidi Fan, Yuhao Yangzong, Yilanda Nihemaiti, Tingting Zhou, Takeshi Yamakawa, Jing Zhao, Leifeng Wang
item.dc:title: Photoredox/Palladium Promoted Selective Dialkylation of α,α′‐Amino C(sp3)H Bonds from N,N‐Dimethyl‐1‐sulfonyl Enamines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Effect of HMPA on the Ground and Excited State Reactivity of SmI2
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A tandem neural network directly solves the multivalued inverse problem of extracting semiconductor parameters from transistor measurements. Trained on only 1000 simulations, the network infers six material parameters (e.g., defect states, carrier concentration, mobility) in under 1 ms, demonstrating a broadly applicable framework for semiconductor analysis and enabling real‐time feedback for autonomous experimentation and digital twins.
Samarium Diiodide (SmI2) is a privileged reducing agent in organic synthesis. Tunability of reactivity and selectivity of SmI2 through additives made this reagent an attractive choice for organic chemists. Present investigation showed contrasting effects of hexamethylphosphoramide (HMPA) on the ground and excited states of reactions of SmI2 with haloalkanes. While HMPA enhances the rate of reduction of haloalkanes in the ground state, a deleterious impact was found for excited‐state reactions. Detailed investigations with kinetics, element effect, known BDE of CH bonds, and computed CX bond distance indicated that while ground state reactivity is governed by stability of radicals and weakening of CX bond due to HMPA and SmI2 coordination, excited state reactions are predominantly governed by inner sphere nature of electron transfer. The critical role of HMPA in facilitating the reduction of haloalkanes was confirmed using density functional theory‐based calculations.
item.content:encoded:
A tandem neural network directly solves the multivalued inverse problem of extracting semiconductor parameters from transistor measurements. Trained on only 1000 simulations, the network infers six material parameters (e.g., defect states, carrier concentration, mobility) in under 1 ms, demonstrating a broadly applicable framework for semiconductor analysis and enabling real-time feedback for autonomous experimentation and digital twins.


Samarium Diiodide (SmI2) is a privileged reducing agent in organic synthesis. Tunability of reactivity and selectivity of SmI2 through additives made this reagent an attractive choice for organic chemists. Present investigation showed contrasting effects of hexamethylphosphoramide (HMPA) on the ground and excited states of reactions of SmI2 with haloalkanes. While HMPA enhances the rate of reduction of haloalkanes in the ground state, a deleterious impact was found for excited-state reactions. Detailed investigations with kinetics, element effect, known BDE of CH bonds, and computed CX bond distance indicated that while ground state reactivity is governed by stability of radicals and weakening of CX bond due to HMPA and SmI2 coordination, excited state reactions are predominantly governed by inner sphere nature of electron transfer. The critical role of HMPA in facilitating the reduction of haloalkanes was confirmed using density functional theory-based calculations.
item.dc:creator: Suranjan De, Athira Jayasankar, Sooraj Kunnikuruvan, Sandeepan Maity
item.dc:title: Effect of HMPA on the Ground and Excited State Reactivity of SmI2

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed [3 + 2] Annulation of Spirovinylcyclopropyl Oxindoles: A Ring‐Opening/Annulation Cascade to 1,3‐Dispirocyclopentane Skeletons
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A palladium‐catalyzed cascade reaction has been developed for the construction of dispiro cyclopentane‐1,2’‐indanedione‐3,3’’‐indoline frameworks. 21 examples of dispiro derivatives were prepared. This method features mild conditions, simple operation, facile scalability, and broad substrate compatibility.
A palladium‐catalyzed cascade reaction has been developed for the construction of dispiro cyclopentane‐1,2’‐indanedione‐3,3’’‐indoline frameworks. This strategy resorts to the unique reactivity of stable spirovinylcyclopropyl oxindoles and 2‐arylidene‐1,3‐indanediones, enabling a ring‐opening followed by a [3 + 2] annulation in a single operation. The protocol is distinguished by its mild conditions, high efficiency, as well as broad substrate generality, providing a versatile access to these valuable molecular architectures.
item.content:encoded:
A palladium-catalyzed cascade reaction has been developed for the construction of dispiro cyclopentane-1,2’-indanedione-3,3’’-indoline frameworks. 21 examples of dispiro derivatives were prepared. This method features mild conditions, simple operation, facile scalability, and broad substrate compatibility.


A palladium-catalyzed cascade reaction has been developed for the construction of dispiro cyclopentane-1,2’-indanedione-3,3’’-indoline frameworks. This strategy resorts to the unique reactivity of stable spirovinylcyclopropyl oxindoles and 2-arylidene-1,3-indanediones, enabling a ring-opening followed by a [3 + 2] annulation in a single operation. The protocol is distinguished by its mild conditions, high efficiency, as well as broad substrate generality, providing a versatile access to these valuable molecular architectures.
item.dc:creator: Yingpeng Su, Fang Shi, Guiyan Cao, Haiyang He, Shufang Zhang, Miao Xiang, Yuntong Lu, Xiaolong Zhao, Congde Huo, Xiaolei Wang
item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed [3 + 2] Annulation of Spirovinylcyclopropyl Oxindoles: A Ring‐Opening/Annulation Cascade to 1,3‐Dispirocyclopentane Skeletons

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Advances in CN Condensation Strategies for Benzimidazole Synthesis: A Synthetic Methodology Review
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Benzimidazoles are well known structural motifs that are used in material sciences, medicines, and pharmaceuticals. They also act as an important class of heterocyclic species for the transformation into various useful molecules as a complement to previous protocols of CH functionalization for the synthesis and application of benzimidazoles. Therefore, this review is targeted to the direct synthesis, mechanism and CH functionalization of benzimidazoles using various transition metal catalyst. Moreover, hypervalent iodine, radical‐ mediated and metal‐free approaches were also highlighted.
Ongoing advances in synthetic methods have greatly expanded access to benzimidazole derivatives through innovative catalytic and non‐catalytic approaches. This review focuses on recent advances in copper‐catalyzed reactions, condensation processes, noble‐metal, and first‐row transition‐metal catalysis, as well as metal‐free, hypervalent iodine‐mediated, and radical‐based techniques for constructing benzimidazole scaffolds. This review highlights significant contributions to mechanistic insights, catalyst systems, and substrate scope, highlighting methods that enable one‐pot transformations, direct CH functionalization, and aiming to provide a conceptual summary that will be useful to researchers and inspire further developments in the rapidly growing medicinal field and advanced synthetic design.
item.content:encoded:
Benzimidazoles are well known structural motifs that are used in material sciences, medicines, and pharmaceuticals. They also act as an important class of heterocyclic species for the transformation into various useful molecules as a complement to previous protocols of CH functionalization for the synthesis and application of benzimidazoles. Therefore, this review is targeted to the direct synthesis, mechanism and CH functionalization of benzimidazoles using various transition metal catalyst. Moreover, hypervalent iodine, radical- mediated and metal-free approaches were also highlighted.


Ongoing advances in synthetic methods have greatly expanded access to benzimidazole derivatives through innovative catalytic and non-catalytic approaches. This review focuses on recent advances in copper-catalyzed reactions, condensation processes, noble-metal, and first-row transition-metal catalysis, as well as metal-free, hypervalent iodine-mediated, and radical-based techniques for constructing benzimidazole scaffolds. This review highlights significant contributions to mechanistic insights, catalyst systems, and substrate scope, highlighting methods that enable one-pot transformations, direct CH functionalization, and aiming to provide a conceptual summary that will be useful to researchers and inspire further developments in the rapidly growing medicinal field and advanced synthetic design.

item.dc:title: Advances in CN Condensation Strategies for Benzimidazole Synthesis: A Synthetic Methodology Review

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Polysubstituted Furans via a [3+2] Cycloaddition Reaction of β‐Ketosulfones With Styrenes Under Copper Catalysis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we demonstrate that the reaction of β‐ketosulfone with p‐methoxystyrene in DMSO at 120°C under air conditions for 12 h can effectively produce a series of furan derivatives, with a maximum yield of 84%. A total of 27 target compounds were obtained.
Herein, we demonstrate that the reaction of β‐ketosulfone with p‐methoxystyrene in DMSO at 120°C under air conditions for 12 h can effectively produce a series of furan derivatives, with a maximum yield of 84%. A total of 27 target compounds were obtained, demonstrating a wide range of substrate applicability and excellent functional group tolerance, thus offering a straightforward and effective method for synthesizing furan derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
Herein, we demonstrate that the reaction of β-ketosulfone with p-methoxystyrene in DMSO at 120°C under air conditions for 12 h can effectively produce a series of furan derivatives, with a maximum yield of 84%. A total of 27 target compounds were obtained.


Herein, we demonstrate that the reaction of β-ketosulfone with p-methoxystyrene in DMSO at 120°C under air conditions for 12 h can effectively produce a series of furan derivatives, with a maximum yield of 84%. A total of 27 target compounds were obtained, demonstrating a wide range of substrate applicability and excellent functional group tolerance, thus offering a straightforward and effective method for synthesizing furan derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Guirong You, Kaiqi Wang, Jian Sun, Rui Xu, Chengcai Xia, Liqiang Hao
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Polysubstituted Furans via a [3+2] Cycloaddition Reaction of β‐Ketosulfones With Styrenes Under Copper Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Thiocyanato/Selenocyanato‐Containing Z‐Vinyl Sulfides Via Tf2O/DMSO‐Promoted Sulfenofunctionalization of Alkynes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A practical sulfonium salt‐mediated regio‐ and stereoselective thio/selenocyanation of alkynes using inexpensive NH4SCN and KSeCN as the thiocyanating and selenocyanating agents has been readily developed through a one‐pot, two‐step process under simple conditions.
In this work, we developed a practical sulfonium‐salt mediated difunctionalization of alkynes with NH4SCN/KSeCN. This method enables the effective synthesis of various thiocyanato/selenocyanato (SCN/SeCN)‐containing vinyl sulfides using DMSO as a readily available methylthio source. The reaction exhibits excellent regio‐ and Z‐selectivity with a broad substrate scope and high functional group compatibility. Gram‐scale experiments and late‐stage functionalization further make this protocol quite attractive in organic synthesis.
item.content:encoded:
A practical sulfonium salt-mediated regio- and stereoselective thio/selenocyanation of alkynes using inexpensive NH4SCN and KSeCN as the thiocyanating and selenocyanating agents has been readily developed through a one-pot, two-step process under simple conditions.


In this work, we developed a practical sulfonium-salt mediated difunctionalization of alkynes with NH4SCN/KSeCN. This method enables the effective synthesis of various thiocyanato/selenocyanato (SCN/SeCN)-containing vinyl sulfides using DMSO as a readily available methylthio source. The reaction exhibits excellent regio- and Z-selectivity with a broad substrate scope and high functional group compatibility. Gram-scale experiments and late-stage functionalization further make this protocol quite attractive in organic synthesis.
item.dc:creator: Zheng‐Wu Tan, Hong Yang, Xiang‐Qin Xu, Li‐Hua Yang, Long‐Yong Xie
item.dc:title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Thiocyanato/Selenocyanato‐Containing Z‐Vinyl Sulfides Via Tf2O/DMSO‐Promoted Sulfenofunctionalization of Alkynes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Regio‐ and Stereoselective Iodochlorination/Cross‐Coupling Strategy for Isomeric Trifluoromethylated Tetrasubstituted Alkenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Trifluoromethylated tetrasubstituted alkenes are valuable motifs but challenging to synthesize stereoselectively. Here, we report a regio‐ and stereoselective iodochlorination of trifluoromethylated alkynes, providing versatile dihalides. Sequential cross‐coupling reactions enabled access to distinct isomers, offering a general strategy for the synthesis of complex trifluoromethylated alkenes for pharmaceutical and materials applications.
Trifluoromethylated tetrasubstituted alkenes are valuable motifs in biologically active compounds and functional materials, but their stereoselective synthesis remains challenging owing to the limited number of methods available. In this study, we describe a highly regio‐ and stereoselective iodochlorination of trifluoromethylated internal alkynes using N‐iodosuccinimide and lithium chloride in an acetic acid/tetrahydrofuran mixed solvent system. The resulting vicinal dihalides serve as versatile intermediates for sequential cross‐coupling reactions, thereby providing efficient and stereodefined routes to densely substituted alkenes. Importantly, judicious choice of both substrates and coupling partners enables the selective preparation of distinct alkene isomers, demonstrating the method's flexibility. This approach offers a straightforward and general strategy for constructing complex trifluoromethylated alkenes, with potential applications in medicinal chemistry, agrochemicals, and materials science, where precise control of alkene geometry is essential.
item.content:encoded:
Trifluoromethylated tetrasubstituted alkenes are valuable motifs but challenging to synthesize stereoselectively. Here, we report a regio- and stereoselective iodochlorination of trifluoromethylated alkynes, providing versatile dihalides. Sequential cross-coupling reactions enabled access to distinct isomers, offering a general strategy for the synthesis of complex trifluoromethylated alkenes for pharmaceutical and materials applications.


Trifluoromethylated tetrasubstituted alkenes are valuable motifs in biologically active compounds and functional materials, but their stereoselective synthesis remains challenging owing to the limited number of methods available. In this study, we describe a highly regio- and stereoselective iodochlorination of trifluoromethylated internal alkynes using N-iodosuccinimide and lithium chloride in an acetic acid/tetrahydrofuran mixed solvent system. The resulting vicinal dihalides serve as versatile intermediates for sequential cross-coupling reactions, thereby providing efficient and stereodefined routes to densely substituted alkenes. Importantly, judicious choice of both substrates and coupling partners enables the selective preparation of distinct alkene isomers, demonstrating the method's flexibility. This approach offers a straightforward and general strategy for constructing complex trifluoromethylated alkenes, with potential applications in medicinal chemistry, agrochemicals, and materials science, where precise control of alkene geometry is essential.

item.dc:title: Regio‐ and Stereoselective Iodochlorination/Cross‐Coupling Strategy for Isomeric Trifluoromethylated Tetrasubstituted Alkenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Regioselective C8–H Sulfonamidation of Isoquinolones With Sulfonyl Azides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We report a Ru(II)‐catalyzed, directing‐group‐assisted regioselective C8–H sulfonamidation of N‐aryl and N‐alkylisoquinolones using sulfonyl azides. The transformation proceeds under mild conditions with excellent regioselectivity. Aryl and polyaromatic sulfonyl azides delivered products in good to excellent yields. This protocol provides access to pharmaceutically relevant C8‐sulfonamidated isoquinolones.
We report a Ru(II)‐catalyzed, directing‐group‐assisted regioselective C8–H sulfonamidation of N‐aryl and N‐alkylisoquinolones using sulfonyl azides. The transformation proceeds under mild conditions with excellent regioselectivity. Aryl and polyaromatic sulfonyl azides delivered products in good to excellent yields. This protocol affords access to pharmaceutically relevant C8‐sulfonamidated isoquinolones.
item.content:encoded:
We report a Ru(II)-catalyzed, directing-group-assisted regioselective C8–H sulfonamidation of N-aryl and N-alkylisoquinolones using sulfonyl azides. The transformation proceeds under mild conditions with excellent regioselectivity. Aryl and polyaromatic sulfonyl azides delivered products in good to excellent yields. This protocol provides access to pharmaceutically relevant C8-sulfonamidated isoquinolones.


We report a Ru(II)-catalyzed, directing-group-assisted regioselective C8–H sulfonamidation of N-aryl and N-alkylisoquinolones using sulfonyl azides. The transformation proceeds under mild conditions with excellent regioselectivity. Aryl and polyaromatic sulfonyl azides delivered products in good to excellent yields. This protocol affords access to pharmaceutically relevant C8-sulfonamidated isoquinolones.
item.dc:creator: Suruchi Kumari, Abhishek Kumar Shaw, Anushka Rastogi, Ruchir Kant, Dipankar Koley
item.dc:title: Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Regioselective C8–H Sulfonamidation of Isoquinolones With Sulfonyl Azides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: The Rapid Construction and Biological Evaluation of Densely Substituted Carbazoles Via I2/H2O‐Mediated Green Approach
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A greener catalytic route to carbazoles bridges sustainable synthesis with biological activity.
We report a green and efficient iodine‐catalysed cascade reaction of indoles with α‐bromoacetophenones, enabling the rapid and highly regioselective construction of diverse carbazole scaffolds. Remarkably, this metal‐ and oxidant‐free protocol operates with only 5 mol% iodine in water, converting unprotected and protected indoles to carbazoles in excellent yields. A series of carbazole derivatives were synthesised under mild, operationally simple conditions, demonstrating broad functional group tolerance and excellent scalability to gram‐scale synthesis. The biological evaluation revealed that among the compounds tested, six compounds showed cell viability below 50% in A549 lung cancer cells and whereas nine compounds in SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells. Selectivity index calculations further indicated that one compound displayed moderate selectivity towards A549 cells, whereas five compounds showed preferential cytotoxicity against SKOV3 cells, highlighting the promising therapeutic potential of these scaffolds. Collectively, these findings underscore the medicinal relevance of the synthesised carbazole frameworks and the potential of this cost‐effective, chemo‐divergent and sustainable strategy for generating structurally complex, bioactive carbazole cores in anti‐cancer drug development.
item.content:encoded:
A greener catalytic route to carbazoles bridges sustainable synthesis with biological activity.


We report a green and efficient iodine-catalysed cascade reaction of indoles with α-bromoacetophenones, enabling the rapid and highly regioselective construction of diverse carbazole scaffolds. Remarkably, this metal- and oxidant-free protocol operates with only 5 mol% iodine in water, converting unprotected and protected indoles to carbazoles in excellent yields. A series of carbazole derivatives were synthesised under mild, operationally simple conditions, demonstrating broad functional group tolerance and excellent scalability to gram-scale synthesis. The biological evaluation revealed that among the compounds tested, six compounds showed cell viability below 50% in A549 lung cancer cells and whereas nine compounds in SKOV3 ovarian cancer cells. Selectivity index calculations further indicated that one compound displayed moderate selectivity towards A549 cells, whereas five compounds showed preferential cytotoxicity against SKOV3 cells, highlighting the promising therapeutic potential of these scaffolds. Collectively, these findings underscore the medicinal relevance of the synthesised carbazole frameworks and the potential of this cost-effective, chemo-divergent and sustainable strategy for generating structurally complex, bioactive carbazole cores in anti-cancer drug development.

item.dc:title: The Rapid Construction and Biological Evaluation of Densely Substituted Carbazoles Via I2/H2O‐Mediated Green Approach

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Formal Nitrogen Atom Insertion into N‐Aroyl‐7‐Azaindoles Toward Blocked Isocyanates for the Synthesis of Unsymmetrical Ureas
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
N‐Phenyl‐1H‐pyrrolo[2,3‐b]pyridine‐1‐carboxamides, a new class of blocked isocyanates with balanced stability and reactivity, were synthesized by the reaction of TMSN3 with N‐aroyl‐7‐azaindoles. Transamidation of these carboxamides with various amines was successfully achieved to produce unsymmetrical ureas.
The interaction of TMSN3 with N‐aroyl‐7‐azaindoles in the presence of an acid catalyst resulted in a formal nitrogen atom insertion, leading to the synthesis of N‐phenyl‐1H‐pyrrolo[2,3‐b]pyridine‐1‐carboxamides, a novel class of blocked isocyanates. Mechanistic investigations demonstrated that the key step in the reaction was the Curtius rearrangement, which involves a sequence of elimination and addition processes of the 7‐azaindole moiety. Furthermore, transamidation of these carboxamides with various amines was successfully conducted to produce unsymmetrical ureas.
item.content:encoded:
N-Phenyl-1H-pyrrolo[2,3-b]pyridine-1-carboxamides, a new class of blocked isocyanates with balanced stability and reactivity, were synthesized by the reaction of TMSN3 with N-aroyl-7-azaindoles. Transamidation of these carboxamides with various amines was successfully achieved to produce unsymmetrical ureas.


The interaction of TMSN3 with N-aroyl-7-azaindoles in the presence of an acid catalyst resulted in a formal nitrogen atom insertion, leading to the synthesis of N-phenyl-1H-pyrrolo[2,3-b]pyridine-1-carboxamides, a novel class of blocked isocyanates. Mechanistic investigations demonstrated that the key step in the reaction was the Curtius rearrangement, which involves a sequence of elimination and addition processes of the 7-azaindole moiety. Furthermore, transamidation of these carboxamides with various amines was successfully conducted to produce unsymmetrical ureas.
item.dc:creator: Mei Zhang, Meng‐Meng Xu, Yu‐Xiang Wu, Yong‐Jun Yuan, Shun‐Jun Ji, Xiao‐Ping Xu
item.dc:title: A Formal Nitrogen Atom Insertion into N‐Aroyl‐7‐Azaindoles Toward Blocked Isocyanates for the Synthesis of Unsymmetrical Ureas

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanistic and Reactivity Insights into Diboron‐Mediated NN and NN Bond Scission
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review highlights the emerging ability of diboron reagents to cleave both π‐bonded (N=N) and σ‐bonded (N—N) nitrogen–nitrogen linkages, integrating mechanistic understanding with recent synthetic applications.
Diboron reagents have emerged as versatile reducing agents capable of transforming a broad range of functional groups through pathways like hydrogen‐ or metal–hydride‐based reductions. Moreover, the strong thermodynamic driving force for BO bond formation enables efficient cleavage of XO bonds and straightforward deoxygenation without the need for external hydrogen sources such as water or alcohol. This intrinsic reactivity can be further enhanced by bases or Lewis‐basic additives, including pyridines, which promote BB bond activation and broaden the scope of transformations accessible under mild conditions. The effectiveness of diboron‐mediated XO bond cleavage naturally raises the question of whether these reagents might also promote the scission of NN bondsa transformation of increasing interest due to the prevalence of NN motifs in synthetic intermediates, pharmaceuticals, and nitrogen‐rich frameworks. This review outlines current understanding of diboron‐mediated NN and NN bond cleavage and unifies mechanistic and reactivity insights to guide future advances in diboron‐based synthetic transformations.
item.content:encoded:
This review highlights the emerging ability of diboron reagents to cleave both π-bonded (N=N) and σ-bonded (N—N) nitrogen–nitrogen linkages, integrating mechanistic understanding with recent synthetic applications.


Diboron reagents have emerged as versatile reducing agents capable of transforming a broad range of functional groups through pathways like hydrogen- or metal–hydride-based reductions. Moreover, the strong thermodynamic driving force for BO bond formation enables efficient cleavage of XO bonds and straightforward deoxygenation without the need for external hydrogen sources such as water or alcohol. This intrinsic reactivity can be further enhanced by bases or Lewis-basic additives, including pyridines, which promote BB bond activation and broaden the scope of transformations accessible under mild conditions. The effectiveness of diboron-mediated XO bond cleavage naturally raises the question of whether these reagents might also promote the scission of NN bondsa transformation of increasing interest due to the prevalence of NN motifs in synthetic intermediates, pharmaceuticals, and nitrogen-rich frameworks. This review outlines current understanding of diboron-mediated NN and NN bond cleavage and unifies mechanistic and reactivity insights to guide future advances in diboron-based synthetic transformations.
item.dc:creator: Raúl Valderrama‐Callejón, Inés Alonso, M. Belén Cid
item.dc:title: Mechanistic and Reactivity Insights into Diboron‐Mediated NN and NN Bond Scission

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Multivalency‐Enhanced IDO Inhibition Based on 1‐Methyl‐Tryptophan‐Perfunctionalized Pillar[5]arene Nanoparticles for Combined Cancer Therapy
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A nanoparticle (MP5L@DOX) is self‐assembled from 1‐methyl‐tryptophan (1‐MT)‐perfunctionalized pillararene conjugated with a lactose‐derived ligand, encapsulating DOX, whose multivalent scaffold potentiates Indoleamine 2,3‐dioxygenase (IDO) inhibition, triggering immunogenic cell death (ICD) and potentiating IDO inhibition to achieve potent antitumor efficacy and robust antitumor immunity against hepatoma in vivo.
Here, we constructed nanoparticles (MP5L@DOX), which are self‐assembled from 1‐methyl‐tryptophan (1‐MT)‐perfunctionalized pillararene conjugated with a lactose‐derived ligand, with DOX encapsulated within the supramolecular architecture to enable receptor‐mediated cellular uptake and pH/esterase dual‐responsive drug release. The multivalent display of 1‐MT on the pillararene scaffold enhances its Indoleamine 2,3‐dioxygenase (IDO)‐inhibitory potency compared to its free counterpart. In vitro, MP5L@DOX is internalized by asialoglycoprotein receptor (ASGPR)‐overexpressing HepG2 cells, simultaneously inducing immunogenic cell death (ICD) and suppressing IDO activity. In a murine hepatoma model, MP5L@DOX inhibits tumor growth, mitigates systemic toxicity, and promotes dendritic cell maturation and CD8+ T‐cell infiltration, activating an antitumor immune response. This work demonstrates a multivalency‐based supramolecular strategy to potentiate IDO inhibition and provides a rationale for designing combined nanomedicines against immunosuppressive tumors.
item.content:encoded:
A nanoparticle (MP5L@DOX) is self-assembled from 1-methyl-tryptophan (1-MT)-perfunctionalized pillararene conjugated with a lactose-derived ligand, encapsulating DOX, whose multivalent scaffold potentiates Indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO) inhibition, triggering immunogenic cell death (ICD) and potentiating IDO inhibition to achieve potent antitumor efficacy and robust antitumor immunity against hepatoma in vivo.


Here, we constructed nanoparticles (MP5L@DOX), which are self-assembled from 1-methyl-tryptophan (1-MT)-perfunctionalized pillararene conjugated with a lactose-derived ligand, with DOX encapsulated within the supramolecular architecture to enable receptor-mediated cellular uptake and pH/esterase dual-responsive drug release. The multivalent display of 1-MT on the pillararene scaffold enhances its Indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO)-inhibitory potency compared to its free counterpart. In vitro, MP5L@DOX is internalized by asialoglycoprotein receptor (ASGPR)-overexpressing HepG2 cells, simultaneously inducing immunogenic cell death (ICD) and suppressing IDO activity. In a murine hepatoma model, MP5L@DOX inhibits tumor growth, mitigates systemic toxicity, and promotes dendritic cell maturation and CD8+ T-cell infiltration, activating an antitumor immune response. This work demonstrates a multivalency-based supramolecular strategy to potentiate IDO inhibition and provides a rationale for designing combined nanomedicines against immunosuppressive tumors.
item.dc:creator: Xiaojiao Di, Wenkang Zhao, Zelong Chen, Biqing Chen, Yinghua Lv, Zhichao Pei, Yuxin Pei
item.dc:title: Multivalency‐Enhanced IDO Inhibition Based on 1‐Methyl‐Tryptophan‐Perfunctionalized Pillar[5]arene Nanoparticles for Combined Cancer Therapy

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Versatile Photocatalytic Oxidation Enabled by a Tetraphenylethylene‐Based Supramolecular System
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A tetraphenylethylene‐based supramolecular system enables efficient photocatalytic oxidation under mild conditions. Aryl sulfides are converted to sulfoxides (99%) via 1O2, and phenylboronic acids to phenols (90%) via O2•−. Lignin degradation reaches 82%, highlighting a versatile efficient reactive oxygen species‐driven photocatalytic platform.
Artificial systems that efficiently utilize light offer a viable approach for photocatalytic oxidation under mild conditions. Herein, the photocatalytic performance of a tetraphenylethylene‐based supramolecular system is investigated. The assembled system (TPEC/CTAB‐ESY‐SR101) enables multiple oxidation reactions with high efficiency. Aryl sulfides are selectively converted to sulfoxides in up to 99% yield via singlet oxygen (1O2), while phenylboronic acids are oxidized to phenols in up to 90% yield, predominantly mediated by superoxide radicals (O2•−). In addition, lignin photodegradation is significantly enhanced from 35% for TPEC alone to 82% in the assembled system. This work demonstrates a versatile supramolecular platform that integrates efficient reactive oxygen species generation with diverse photocatalytic oxidation reactions, providing a practical strategy for solar‐driven oxidation chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
A tetraphenylethylene-based supramolecular system enables efficient photocatalytic oxidation under mild conditions. Aryl sulfides are converted to sulfoxides (99%) via 1O2, and phenylboronic acids to phenols (90%) via O2 •−. Lignin degradation reaches 82%, highlighting a versatile efficient reactive oxygen species-driven photocatalytic platform.


Artificial systems that efficiently utilize light offer a viable approach for photocatalytic oxidation under mild conditions. Herein, the photocatalytic performance of a tetraphenylethylene-based supramolecular system is investigated. The assembled system (TPEC/CTAB-ESY-SR101) enables multiple oxidation reactions with high efficiency. Aryl sulfides are selectively converted to sulfoxides in up to 99% yield via singlet oxygen (1O2), while phenylboronic acids are oxidized to phenols in up to 90% yield, predominantly mediated by superoxide radicals (O2 •−). In addition, lignin photodegradation is significantly enhanced from 35% for TPEC alone to 82% in the assembled system. This work demonstrates a versatile supramolecular platform that integrates efficient reactive oxygen species generation with diverse photocatalytic oxidation reactions, providing a practical strategy for solar-driven oxidation chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Qiaona Zhang, Qing Liu, Jinchen Li, Long Tang, Tangxin Xiao
item.dc:title: Versatile Photocatalytic Oxidation Enabled by a Tetraphenylethylene‐Based Supramolecular System

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Iminodirhodium‐ and Iminocopper‐Catalyzed CH Activation: Chemoselective Oxidation of Alcohols and Aldehydes via Intermolecular Hydride Abstraction
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Chemoselective hydride abstractor iminometalloids, derived from O‐mesylacetoxime (MAO) using Rh2(II/II) or Cu(I) catalysts, can activate the C–Hα bonds of alcohols and aldehydes ultimately forming ketones, carboxylic acids, and esters even in the presence of C–H bonds with comparable bond strengths.
Iminodirhodium and iminocopper electrophilic intermediates, generated in situ from O‐mesylacetoxime using catalytic Rh2(esp)2 and Cu(I)Br, respectively, are versatile reagents for selective intermolecular CH activation. These reagents oxidize alcohols and aldehydes with the convenience and mildness of contemporary oxidants while improving atom‐efficiency, isolation, and economy. Mechanistic studies suggest an electrophilic, α‐oxy hydride abstraction thus establishing these iminometallic species as a new class of hydride abstractors.
item.content:encoded:
Chemoselective hydride abstractor iminometalloids, derived from O-mesylacetoxime (MAO) using Rh2(II/II) or Cu(I) catalysts, can activate the C–Hα bonds of alcohols and aldehydes ultimately forming ketones, carboxylic acids, and esters even in the presence of C–H bonds with comparable bond strengths.


Iminodirhodium and iminocopper electrophilic intermediates, generated in situ from O-mesylacetoxime using catalytic Rh2(esp)2 and Cu(I)Br, respectively, are versatile reagents for selective intermolecular CH activation. These reagents oxidize alcohols and aldehydes with the convenience and mildness of contemporary oxidants while improving atom-efficiency, isolation, and economy. Mechanistic studies suggest an electrophilic, α-oxy hydride abstraction thus establishing these iminometallic species as a new class of hydride abstractors.
item.dc:creator: Raghunath Reddy Anugu, John R. Falck, Adeniyi M. Adebesin
item.dc:title: Iminodirhodium‐ and Iminocopper‐Catalyzed CH Activation: Chemoselective Oxidation of Alcohols and Aldehydes via Intermolecular Hydride Abstraction

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Short Total Synthesis of the Daucane Sesquiterpenoid Ferutinin
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Only nine steps were required to access the bioactive daucane sesquiterpene ester ferutinin (1) from 3‐methyl‐2‐cyclopentenone (3) and allyl p‐tolyl sulfoxide. A one‐pot 1,4‐addition/aldol sequence was used to rapidly provide diene 2 (TBS = tert‐butyldimethylsilyl), ring‐closing metathesis of which followed by alkylation and acylation gave rise to the natural product 1 in high overall yield.
A straightforward total synthesis of the bioactive daucane sesquiterpene ester ferutinin is reported. Starting from 3‐methyl‐2‐cyclopentenone and allyl p‐tolyl sulfoxide, only nine steps were required to access the target sesquiterpenoid in 22% overall yield. A one‐pot sequential conjugate addition/aldol reaction to set up a trisubstituted cyclopentanone moiety and a ring‐closing metathesis to generate the hydroazulene core were used as the key transformations. Addition of isopropyllithium completed the daucane skeleton and eventually led to the sesquiterpene jaeschkeanadiol. Finally, esterification of this diol gave rise to ferutinin.
item.content:encoded:
Only nine steps were required to access the bioactive daucane sesquiterpene ester ferutinin (1) from 3-methyl-2-cyclopentenone (3) and allyl p-tolyl sulfoxide. A one-pot 1,4-addition/aldol sequence was used to rapidly provide diene 2 (TBS = tert-butyldimethylsilyl), ring-closing metathesis of which followed by alkylation and acylation gave rise to the natural product 1 in high overall yield.


A straightforward total synthesis of the bioactive daucane sesquiterpene ester ferutinin is reported. Starting from 3-methyl-2-cyclopentenone and allyl p-tolyl sulfoxide, only nine steps were required to access the target sesquiterpenoid in 22% overall yield. A one-pot sequential conjugate addition/aldol reaction to set up a trisubstituted cyclopentanone moiety and a ring-closing metathesis to generate the hydroazulene core were used as the key transformations. Addition of isopropyllithium completed the daucane skeleton and eventually led to the sesquiterpene jaeschkeanadiol. Finally, esterification of this diol gave rise to ferutinin.
item.dc:creator: Christiane Osthaar, Yuzhou Wang, Anne Jäger, Peter Metz
item.dc:title: A Short Total Synthesis of the Daucane Sesquiterpenoid Ferutinin

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Facile Synthesis of Spiro Trifluoromethyl Pyrazolone–Pyrazole Derivatives Through a Base‐Mediated (3+2) Cycloaddition
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
CF3 ‐Pyrazolone. 1,3‐dipolar cycloaddition. Metal free conditions. Good yields. Broad substrate scope.
Herein, we describe a 1,3‐dipolar (3 + 2) cycloaddition between hydrazonoyl chlorides and substituted trifluoromethyl pyrazolones for the synthesis of spirotrifluoromethyl pyrazolone–pyrazole derivatives under transition‐metal‐free conditions. This protocol delivers CF3‐containing pyrazolone frameworks of notable synthetic and biological relevance. The cycloaddition enables the simultaneous formation of new CN and CC bonds, providing the desired spiro products in high yields. Moreover, the reaction demonstrates broad substrate compatibility, underscoring its practicality and synthetic utility.
item.content:encoded:
CF3 -Pyrazolone. 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition. Metal free conditions. Good yields. Broad substrate scope.


Herein, we describe a 1,3-dipolar (3 + 2) cycloaddition between hydrazonoyl chlorides and substituted trifluoromethyl pyrazolones for the synthesis of spirotrifluoromethyl pyrazolone–pyrazole derivatives under transition-metal-free conditions. This protocol delivers CF3-containing pyrazolone frameworks of notable synthetic and biological relevance. The cycloaddition enables the simultaneous formation of new CN and CC bonds, providing the desired spiro products in high yields. Moreover, the reaction demonstrates broad substrate compatibility, underscoring its practicality and synthetic utility.
item.dc:creator: Lingareddy Eeda, Susmitha Jadav, Sai Lokavarapu, Ravindra M. Kumbhare
item.dc:title: Facile Synthesis of Spiro Trifluoromethyl Pyrazolone–Pyrazole Derivatives Through a Base‐Mediated (3+2) Cycloaddition

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Activation of Aryl Halides by Triplet–Triplet Annihilation Upconversion Enabling Coupling Processes Under an Open‐to‐Air Environment
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Triplet–triplet annihilation upconversion (TTA‐UC) performed inside supramolecular viscoelastic gels enables efficient CC and C–heteroatom coupling reactions under green LED irradiation in open‐air conditions. The gel network restricts oxygen diffusion, preserves triplet states, and allows aryl halide activation through upconverted excited states, providing a robust and recyclable confined photoredox platform.
Herein, several CC and C–heteroatom coupling reactions were successfully achieved by combining triplet–triplet annihilation upconversion (TTA‐UC) photochemistry with supramolecular viscoelastic gels as confined media under green‐light LED irradiation. Unlike conventional TTA‐UC, which typically requires strict anaerobic conditions due to efficient oxygen quenching of triplet states, the present methodology operates under open‐to‐air conditions. The gel matrix, made of low‐molecular‐weight gelators, provides a nanostructured microenvironment that restricts oxygen diffusion and minimizes triplet‐oxygen collisional quenching, thereby preserving the efficiency of the upconversion process without additional additives. Using the well‐established PtOEP/DPA sensitizer–annihilator pair embedded within the gel network, aryl halides were activated through a photoinduced single‐electron transfer pathway enabled by the upconverted excited state. The method proved effective for a range of substrates affording the corresponding coupling products in moderate to good yields under mild conditions (ambient temperature, atmospheric pressure, visible light). Rheological and microscopic studies confirmed that the fibrillar gel architecture remains largely intact after irradiation, highlighting the mechanical robustness and recyclability of the confined medium for photoredox transformations.
item.content:encoded:
Triplet–triplet annihilation upconversion (TTA-UC) performed inside supramolecular viscoelastic gels enables efficient CC and C–heteroatom coupling reactions under green LED irradiation in open-air conditions. The gel network restricts oxygen diffusion, preserves triplet states, and allows aryl halide activation through upconverted excited states, providing a robust and recyclable confined photoredox platform.


Herein, several CC and C–heteroatom coupling reactions were successfully achieved by combining triplet–triplet annihilation upconversion (TTA-UC) photochemistry with supramolecular viscoelastic gels as confined media under green-light LED irradiation. Unlike conventional TTA-UC, which typically requires strict anaerobic conditions due to efficient oxygen quenching of triplet states, the present methodology operates under open-to-air conditions. The gel matrix, made of low-molecular-weight gelators, provides a nanostructured microenvironment that restricts oxygen diffusion and minimizes triplet-oxygen collisional quenching, thereby preserving the efficiency of the upconversion process without additional additives. Using the well-established PtOEP/DPA sensitizer–annihilator pair embedded within the gel network, aryl halides were activated through a photoinduced single-electron transfer pathway enabled by the upconverted excited state. The method proved effective for a range of substrates affording the corresponding coupling products in moderate to good yields under mild conditions (ambient temperature, atmospheric pressure, visible light). Rheological and microscopic studies confirmed that the fibrillar gel architecture remains largely intact after irradiation, highlighting the mechanical robustness and recyclability of the confined medium for photoredox transformations.

item.dc:title: Activation of Aryl Halides by Triplet–Triplet Annihilation Upconversion Enabling Coupling Processes Under an Open‐to‐Air Environment

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper/Photoredox‐Catalyzed Asymmetric 1,4‐Arylcyanation of 1,3‐Enynes to Access Chiral Tetrasubstituted Allenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A method for the photoredox/copper dual‐catalyzed asymmetric 1,4‐arylcyanation of 1,3‐enynes via a radical pathway has been established, in which Ar‐DBT+ salts serve as aryl radical precursors, affording diverse chiral tetra‐substituted allenes with moderate enantioselectivity and high chemo‐ and regioselectivity. This strategy operates under mild conditions, exhibits broad substrate scope with excellent functional group tolerance, and delivers chiral tetrasubstituted allenes in one step using both electron‐rich and electron‐deficient aryldibenzothiophenium (Ar‐DBT+) salts.
A method for the photoredox/copper dual‐catalyzed asymmetric 1,4‐arylcyanation of 1,3‐enynes via a radical pathway has been established, in which Ar‐DBT+ salts serve as aryl radical precursors, affording diverse chiral tetrasubstituted allenes with moderate enantioselectivity and high chemo‐ and regioselectivity. This strategy operates under mild conditions, exhibits broad substrate scope with excellent functional group tolerance, and delivers chiral tetra‐substituted allenes in one step using both electron‐rich and electron‐deficient aryldibenzothiophenium (Ar‐DBT+) salts.

This strategy operates under mild conditions, exhibits broad substrate scope with excellent functional group tolerance, and delivers chiral tetrasubstituted allenes in one step using both electron-rich and electron-deficient aryldibenzothiophenium (Ar-DBT+) salts.

This strategy operates under mild conditions, exhibits broad substrate scope with excellent functional group tolerance, and delivers chiral tetra-substituted allenes in one step using both electron-rich and electron-deficient aryldibenzothiophenium (Ar-DBT+) salts.
