item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Metal‐Free Synthesis of Allylic Phosphine Oxides via [2,3]‐Rearrangement/Michael Addition Cascade
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A novel metal‐free cascade synthesis of (E)‐2‐chloro‐allyl‐diarylphosphine oxides directly from propargylic alcohols and chlorodiarylphosphines was developed. This method efficiently combines phosphorylation, [2,3]‐sigmatropic rearrangement, and Michael addition in a single operation, achieving high stereocontrol and efficiency for the synthesis of allylic phosphine oxides.
Allylic phosphorous compounds are a class of important skeletons found in bioactive molecules and ligands. Here, we describe a synthesis of allylic phosphorous compounds from propargylic alcohols and chlorodiarylphosphines via a cascade reaction of OP bond formation/[2,3]‐rearrangement/Michael addition under metal‐free conditions. The reaction conditions are operationally simple and relatively mild, giving 2‐chloro‐allyl‐diarylphosphine oxides in (E)‐configuration selectively.
item.content:encoded:
A novel metal-free cascade synthesis of (E)-2-chloro-allyl-diarylphosphine oxides directly from propargylic alcohols and chlorodiarylphosphines was developed. This method efficiently combines phosphorylation, [2,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement, and Michael addition in a single operation, achieving high stereocontrol and efficiency for the synthesis of allylic phosphine oxides.


Allylic phosphorous compounds are a class of important skeletons found in bioactive molecules and ligands. Here, we describe a synthesis of allylic phosphorous compounds from propargylic alcohols and chlorodiarylphosphines via a cascade reaction of OP bond formation/[2,3]-rearrangement/Michael addition under metal-free conditions. The reaction conditions are operationally simple and relatively mild, giving 2-chloro-allyl-diarylphosphine oxides in (E)-configuration selectively.
item.dc:creator: Fangbing Chen, Junqing Liang, Atteeque Ahmed, Jiefeng Shen, Guoqiang Yang
item.dc:title: Metal‐Free Synthesis of Allylic Phosphine Oxides via [2,3]‐Rearrangement/Michael Addition Cascade

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Stereoselective Synthesis of (E)‐(2‐Bromovinyl)selenoxides Using Solid Calcium Carbide as a Substitute for Gaseous Acetylene
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
(E)‐(2‐Bromovinyl)selenoxides were concisely synthesized by a one‐pot procedure with high stereoselectivity using solid calcium carbide as an alkyne source instead of traditional gaseous acetylene, and solid tetrabutylammonium tribromide as a bromide source instead of traditional liquid bromine.
A concise method for the synthesis of (E)‐(2‐bromovinyl)arylselenoxides using calcium carbide as a solid alkyne source, tetrabutylammonium tribromide as a solid bromine source, benzoyl peroxide as an oxidant and an initiator, and 1,2‐diaryldiselenides as starting materials through a one‐pot procedure is described. The target products were obtained via cascade reactions involving the simultaneous formation of SeC, SeO, and CBr bonds. The salient features for this protocol are the use of inexpensive and easy‐to‐handle solid alkyne source instead of flammable and explosive gaseous acetylene, the use of safe and convenient solid bromine source instead of toxic and corrosive liquid bromine, high stereoselectivity, transition metal‐free conditions, wide functional tolerance, satisfactory yield, and simple workup procedures. The reactions can also be carried out on a gram scale.
item.content:encoded:
(E)-(2-Bromovinyl)selenoxides were concisely synthesized by a one-pot procedure with high stereoselectivity using solid calcium carbide as an alkyne source instead of traditional gaseous acetylene, and solid tetrabutylammonium tribromide as a bromide source instead of traditional liquid bromine.


A concise method for the synthesis of (E)-(2-bromovinyl)arylselenoxides using calcium carbide as a solid alkyne source, tetrabutylammonium tribromide as a solid bromine source, benzoyl peroxide as an oxidant and an initiator, and 1,2-diaryldiselenides as starting materials through a one-pot procedure is described. The target products were obtained via cascade reactions involving the simultaneous formation of SeC, SeO, and CBr bonds. The salient features for this protocol are the use of inexpensive and easy-to-handle solid alkyne source instead of flammable and explosive gaseous acetylene, the use of safe and convenient solid bromine source instead of toxic and corrosive liquid bromine, high stereoselectivity, transition metal-free conditions, wide functional tolerance, satisfactory yield, and simple workup procedures. The reactions can also be carried out on a gram scale.
item.dc:creator: Ting Shao, Botao Wang, Jinhui Yang, Zheng Li
item.dc:title: Highly Stereoselective Synthesis of (E)‐(2‐Bromovinyl)selenoxides Using Solid Calcium Carbide as a Substitute for Gaseous Acetylene

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Alkyl Arylacetates in Cascade Reactions with 2‐Cyanobenzaldehydes for the Synthesis of 3‐Substituted Isoindolinones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Effective synthesis of 3‐substituted isoindolinones is possible through novel cascade reactions of alkyl arylacetates with 2‐cyanobenzaldehydes under mild, basic conditions.
Herein we report the use of alkyl arylacetates as pro‐nucleophiles in cascade reactions with 2‐cyanobenzaldehydes leading to the formation of novel 3‐substituted isoindolinones, a heterocyclic scaffold found in many natural products and bioactive synthetic compounds. The reaction proceeds under very mild conditions, generally affording good to excellent yields. We also demonstrated that diastereoselectivity is dependent on the presence of strong electron‐withdrawing substituents at the aromatic ring of the nucleophile. Kinetic investigations were performed by UV–vis stopped‐flow methods, indicating a clear dependence of the reaction rate on the nucleophilic reactivity of the carbanionic intermediates.
item.content:encoded:
Effective synthesis of 3-substituted isoindolinones is possible through novel cascade reactions of alkyl arylacetates with 2-cyanobenzaldehydes under mild, basic conditions.


Herein we report the use of alkyl arylacetates as pro-nucleophiles in cascade reactions with 2-cyanobenzaldehydes leading to the formation of novel 3-substituted isoindolinones, a heterocyclic scaffold found in many natural products and bioactive synthetic compounds. The reaction proceeds under very mild conditions, generally affording good to excellent yields. We also demonstrated that diastereoselectivity is dependent on the presence of strong electron-withdrawing substituents at the aromatic ring of the nucleophile. Kinetic investigations were performed by UV–vis stopped-flow methods, indicating a clear dependence of the reaction rate on the nucleophilic reactivity of the carbanionic intermediates.
item.dc:creator: Antonia Di Mola, Prabaharan Thiruvengetam, Alessandra Forlenza, Armin R. Ofial, Antonio Massa
item.dc:title: Alkyl Arylacetates in Cascade Reactions with 2‐Cyanobenzaldehydes for the Synthesis of 3‐Substituted Isoindolinones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Construction of Cyclobutane‐Fused Quinolizine Frameworks Through Intramolecular [2 + 2] Cycloaddition of Reissert Compounds from Isoquinolines Catalyzed by an Iridium Visible‐Light Photocatalyst
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An unprecedented [2 + 2] photochemical cycloaddition of Reissert compounds catalyzed by visible‐light photocatalysts under blue light‐emitting diode irradiation (455 nm) is described. The reaction efficiently affords cyclobutane‐fused benzo[b]quinolizinone derivatives in high yields and with moderate to high stereoselectivity. A catalyst loading as low as 0.5 mol% is sufficient to drive the reaction to completion within 1 h in many cases.
Irradiation of 2‐(ω‐alkenoyl)‐1,2‐dihydroisoquinoline‐1‐carbonitriles (Reissert compounds) with blue light‐emitting diodes (λ = 455 nm) in the presence of an iridium‐based photocatalyst efficiently afforded cyclobutane‐fused benzo[b]quinolizinone derivatives in high yields and with moderate to high stereoselectivity. A catalyst loading as low as 0.5 mol% was sufficient to drive the reaction to completion within 1 h in many cases. Mechanistic studies suggest that the transformation proceeds via a stepwise [2 + 2] cycloaddition pathway involving a triplet biradical intermediate.
item.content:encoded:
An unprecedented [2 + 2] photochemical cycloaddition of Reissert compounds catalyzed by visible-light photocatalysts under blue light-emitting diode irradiation (455 nm) is described. The reaction efficiently affords cyclobutane-fused benzo[b]quinolizinone derivatives in high yields and with moderate to high stereoselectivity. A catalyst loading as low as 0.5 mol% is sufficient to drive the reaction to completion within 1 h in many cases.


Irradiation of 2-(ω-alkenoyl)-1,2-dihydroisoquinoline-1-carbonitriles (Reissert compounds) with blue light-emitting diodes (λ = 455 nm) in the presence of an iridium-based photocatalyst efficiently afforded cyclobutane-fused benzo[b]quinolizinone derivatives in high yields and with moderate to high stereoselectivity. A catalyst loading as low as 0.5 mol% was sufficient to drive the reaction to completion within 1 h in many cases. Mechanistic studies suggest that the transformation proceeds via a stepwise [2 + 2] cycloaddition pathway involving a triplet biradical intermediate.
item.dc:creator: Noriyoshi Arai, Tatsuro Imakubo, Nonoka Tajiri
item.dc:title: Construction of Cyclobutane‐Fused Quinolizine Frameworks Through Intramolecular [2 + 2] Cycloaddition of Reissert Compounds from Isoquinolines Catalyzed by an Iridium Visible‐Light Photocatalyst

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Pt/C‐Catalyzed Deuteration of Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons with Suppressed Arene Hydrogenation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An additive‐enabled Pt/C protocol promotes hydrogen–deuterium exchange in fused polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) in D2O/2‐PrOH at 80 °C. Decalin suppresses undesired ring hydrogenation while accelerating on‐ring H–D exchange, furnishing highly deuterated PAHs (PAHs‐dn). This method provides practical access to deuterated π‐architectures and rigid scaffolds for mechanistic and photophysical studies.
Deuterated polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are indispensable for investigating isotope effects in π‐conjugated materials; however, catalytic H–D exchange of readily hydrogenated PAHs is often compromised by competitive ring hydrogenation. This article reports a Pt/C‐catalyzed H–D exchange protocol employing inexpensive D2O as the deuterium source and 2‐propanol as the hydrogen donor under an Ar atmosphere. Additive screening identified decalin as a critical reaction modulator, enabling efficient deuterium incorporation while suppressing the formation of partially hydrogenated byproducts. Under the optimized conditions (80°C, 12 h), ≤97% deuterium incorporation was achieved for anthracene. Additionally, a range of fused PAHs, including 9‐aryl anthracenes, pyrene, triphenylene, and triptycene, were converted to their deuterated analogs in good‐to‐excellent isolated yields with negligible ring hydrogenation. This operationally simple method provides streamlined access to deuterated PAHs for materials‐oriented investigations and isotopic‐labeling applications.
item.content:encoded:
An additive-enabled Pt/C protocol promotes hydrogen–deuterium exchange in fused polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) in D2O/2-PrOH at 80 °C. Decalin suppresses undesired ring hydrogenation while accelerating on-ring H–D exchange, furnishing highly deuterated PAHs (PAHs-d n). This method provides practical access to deuterated π-architectures and rigid scaffolds for mechanistic and photophysical studies.


Deuterated polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are indispensable for investigating isotope effects in π-conjugated materials; however, catalytic H–D exchange of readily hydrogenated PAHs is often compromised by competitive ring hydrogenation. This article reports a Pt/C-catalyzed H–D exchange protocol employing inexpensive D2O as the deuterium source and 2-propanol as the hydrogen donor under an Ar atmosphere. Additive screening identified decalin as a critical reaction modulator, enabling efficient deuterium incorporation while suppressing the formation of partially hydrogenated byproducts. Under the optimized conditions (80°C, 12 h), ≤97% deuterium incorporation was achieved for anthracene. Additionally, a range of fused PAHs, including 9-aryl anthracenes, pyrene, triphenylene, and triptycene, were converted to their deuterated analogs in good-to-excellent isolated yields with negligible ring hydrogenation. This operationally simple method provides streamlined access to deuterated PAHs for materials-oriented investigations and isotopic-labeling applications.
item.dc:creator: Naoya Sakurada, Sae Tomoto, Naoki Oka, Tomohiro Ichikawa, Takashi Shigeta, Hironao Sajiki, Takashi Ikawa
item.dc:title: Pt/C‐Catalyzed Deuteration of Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons with Suppressed Arene Hydrogenation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Overcoming the Reactivity Challenge in Radical Fluoroalkylation of N‐Phenylhydrazones: A Divergent Approach for Substrates Derived from Aliphatic and Aromatic Aldehydes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A photocatalytic method for the C–H perfluoroalkylation of N‐phenylhydrazones is described. The reaction works with perfluoroalkyl iodides and bromides as radical precursors, and the products can be transformed into various nitrogen heterocycles.
A photocatalytic method for the C–H perfluoroalkylation of N‐phenylhydrazones is described. The reaction effectively utilizes both fluoroalkyl iodides and bromides as radical precursors, which enables the introduction of a wide variety of fluoroalkyl fragments. Different reaction conditions were required for hydrazones derived from aliphatic and aromatic aldehydes. The obtained α‐perfluoroalkyl‐substituted hydrazones serve as precursors for the synthesis of diverse heterocycles bearing a fluoroalkyl group.
item.content:encoded:
A photocatalytic method for the C–H perfluoroalkylation of N-phenylhydrazones is described. The reaction works with perfluoroalkyl iodides and bromides as radical precursors, and the products can be transformed into various nitrogen heterocycles.


A photocatalytic method for the C–H perfluoroalkylation of N-phenylhydrazones is described. The reaction effectively utilizes both fluoroalkyl iodides and bromides as radical precursors, which enables the introduction of a wide variety of fluoroalkyl fragments. Different reaction conditions were required for hydrazones derived from aliphatic and aromatic aldehydes. The obtained α-perfluoroalkyl-substituted hydrazones serve as precursors for the synthesis of diverse heterocycles bearing a fluoroalkyl group.
item.dc:creator: German V. Svetlakov, Zakhar M. Rubanov, Vitalij V. Levin, Alexander D. Dilman
item.dc:title: Overcoming the Reactivity Challenge in Radical Fluoroalkylation of N‐Phenylhydrazones: A Divergent Approach for Substrates Derived from Aliphatic and Aromatic Aldehydes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Oxazolyl‐Piperidines Bearing a Quaternary Stereocenter Through a Multicomponent Reaction Involving α‐Isocyanoacetates and Piperidones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Straightforward access to oxazole‐containing piperidines through a multicomponent reaction from α‐isocyanoacetates is described. By expanding the reactivity of these a‐isocyanoacetates from aldehydes to ketones, this process efficiently delivers oxazolyl‐piperidines bearing a quaternary stereocenter as valuable scaffolds relevant to drug discovery.
The synthesis of 5‐alkyloxy‐oxazoles from α‐isocyanoacetates, aldehydes, and amines is well known, yet analogous reactions with ketones remain largely unexplored. Here, we investigate 3‐ and 4‐piperidones, selected for their biological relevance, in a multicomponent reaction with α‐(EWG‐phenyl)‐α‐isocyanoacetates and amines. This adapted multicomponent reaction (MCR) protocol provides an efficient, sustainable, and atom‐economic route to 4‐oxazolyl‐piperidines containing a quaternary stereocenter. The optimized conditions afford the desired heterocycles in good to excellent yields. Overall, this methodology expands the synthetic utility of isocyanoacetate‐based MCRs and delivers valuable scaffolds relevant to drug discovery.
item.content:encoded:
Straightforward access to oxazole-containing piperidines through a multicomponent reaction from α-isocyanoacetates is described. By expanding the reactivity of these a-isocyanoacetates from aldehydes to ketones, this process efficiently delivers oxazolyl-piperidines bearing a quaternary stereocenter as valuable scaffolds relevant to drug discovery.


The synthesis of 5-alkyloxy-oxazoles from α-isocyanoacetates, aldehydes, and amines is well known, yet analogous reactions with ketones remain largely unexplored. Here, we investigate 3- and 4-piperidones, selected for their biological relevance, in a multicomponent reaction with α-(EWG-phenyl)-α-isocyanoacetates and amines. This adapted multicomponent reaction (MCR) protocol provides an efficient, sustainable, and atom-economic route to 4-oxazolyl-piperidines containing a quaternary stereocenter. The optimized conditions afford the desired heterocycles in good to excellent yields. Overall, this methodology expands the synthetic utility of isocyanoacetate-based MCRs and delivers valuable scaffolds relevant to drug discovery.
item.dc:creator: Roshan Chandrakant Kajare, Gilles Argouarch, Nicolas Gouault, Claudia Lalli
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Oxazolyl‐Piperidines Bearing a Quaternary Stereocenter Through a Multicomponent Reaction Involving α‐Isocyanoacetates and Piperidones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible Light or Brønsted Acid Promoted Oxygenation of a PdBn(NNP(O)) Complex
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A benzylpalladium complex with a tridentate NNP ligand containing 2,2′‐bipyridine and a phosphoryl moiety [PdBn(MeNNP(O))] reacted readily with O2 to afford an O2‐inserted complex. The insertion was accelerated using ambient light. By contrast, the reaction with O2 under blue LED irradiation afforded PhCHO as the major oxygenated product. The addition of acids accelerates oxygenation.
A benzylpalladium complex with a tridentate NNP ligand containing 2,2′‐bipyridine and a phosphoryl moiety, [PdBn(MeNNP(O))], was synthesized by reacting [{PdBn(MeNNP(O))}2H](OTf) with DBU. [PdBn(MeNNP(O))] readily reacted with O2 to afford [PdOOBn(MeNNP(O))], which was formed by O2 insertion into the Pd–C(sp3) bond. Although O2 insertion proceeded slowly in the dark, ambient light significantly accelerated insertion. Experimental results and density functional theory calculations showed that O2 insertion proceeded in the dark solely via a radical chain mechanism, whereas insertion under ambient light proceeded via both photoexcitation and radical chain mechanisms. By contrast, the reaction of [PdBn(MeNNP(O))] with O2 under blue LED irradiation afforded PhCHO as the major oxygenated product. Irradiation with blue LED light promoted the homolysis of the Pd–C(sp3) bond to afford a Pd(III) species and benzyl radical. The benzyl radical reacted with O2, and the subsequent hydrogen abstraction and dehydration afforded PhCHO. Moreover, the addition of acid accelerated the reaction of [PdBn(MeNNP(O))] with O2 in the dark, indicating that the phosphoryl moiety assisted the reaction with O2.
item.content:encoded:
A benzylpalladium complex with a tridentate NNP ligand containing 2,2′-bipyridine and a phosphoryl moiety [PdBn(MeNNP(O))] reacted readily with O2 to afford an O2-inserted complex. The insertion was accelerated using ambient light. By contrast, the reaction with O2 under blue LED irradiation afforded PhCHO as the major oxygenated product. The addition of acids accelerates oxygenation.


A benzylpalladium complex with a tridentate NNP ligand containing 2,2′-bipyridine and a phosphoryl moiety, [PdBn(MeNNP(O))], was synthesized by reacting [{PdBn(MeNNP(O))}2H](OTf) with DBU. [PdBn(MeNNP(O))] readily reacted with O2 to afford [PdOOBn(MeNNP(O))], which was formed by O2 insertion into the Pd–C(sp3) bond. Although O2 insertion proceeded slowly in the dark, ambient light significantly accelerated insertion. Experimental results and density functional theory calculations showed that O2 insertion proceeded in the dark solely via a radical chain mechanism, whereas insertion under ambient light proceeded via both photoexcitation and radical chain mechanisms. By contrast, the reaction of [PdBn(MeNNP(O))] with O2 under blue LED irradiation afforded PhCHO as the major oxygenated product. Irradiation with blue LED light promoted the homolysis of the Pd–C(sp3) bond to afford a Pd(III) species and benzyl radical. The benzyl radical reacted with O2, and the subsequent hydrogen abstraction and dehydration afforded PhCHO. Moreover, the addition of acid accelerated the reaction of [PdBn(MeNNP(O))] with O2 in the dark, indicating that the phosphoryl moiety assisted the reaction with O2.
item.dc:creator: Risa Shibai, Yuma Shigehiro, Shogo Sasaki, Yasuyuki Ura
item.dc:title: Visible Light or Brønsted Acid Promoted Oxygenation of a PdBn(NNP(O)) Complex

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Facile Access to Amides via Catalyst‐ and Additive‐Free Transamidation of N‐Acylpyrrole‐Type Amides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A rapid synthesis of amides via transamidation of N‐acylpyrrole‐type amides is reported. This catalyst‐free protocol features mild reaction conditions, operational simplicity, and a broad substrate scope. Notably, hydroxylamines, which represent a class of nucleophiles rarely utilized in transamidation reactions, were successfully accommodated. The present strategy not only enriches the existing methodologies for amide synthesis but also expands the application scope of catalyst‐free transamidation reactions.
Amides are key structural units in organic synthesis, essential for the efficient production of many pharmaceuticals and industrial chemicals. Transamidation enables one‐pot conversion between different amides. However, conventional methods are often hampered by toxic/corrosive reagents, strong acids/bases, or metal‐mediated conditions, which can lead to undesired hydrolysis. Here, a catalyst‐ and additive‐free transamidation of N‐acylpyrrole‐type amides was developed. This method features mild conditions, operational simplicity, broad substrate scope, and excellent functional group compatibility.
item.content:encoded:
A rapid synthesis of amides via transamidation of N-acylpyrrole-type amides is reported. This catalyst-free protocol features mild reaction conditions, operational simplicity, and a broad substrate scope. Notably, hydroxylamines, which represent a class of nucleophiles rarely utilized in transamidation reactions, were successfully accommodated. The present strategy not only enriches the existing methodologies for amide synthesis but also expands the application scope of catalyst-free transamidation reactions.


Amides are key structural units in organic synthesis, essential for the efficient production of many pharmaceuticals and industrial chemicals. Transamidation enables one-pot conversion between different amides. However, conventional methods are often hampered by toxic/corrosive reagents, strong acids/bases, or metal-mediated conditions, which can lead to undesired hydrolysis. Here, a catalyst- and additive-free transamidation of N-acylpyrrole-type amides was developed. This method features mild conditions, operational simplicity, broad substrate scope, and excellent functional group compatibility.
item.dc:creator: Peng‐Fei Yang, Man‐Li Feng, Li Li, Xiu‐Li Zhang
item.dc:title: Facile Access to Amides via Catalyst‐ and Additive‐Free Transamidation of N‐Acylpyrrole‐Type Amides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of 2‐Aza‐Bicyclo[4.3.0]nonane Derivatives Related to the Camporidines via Intramolecular [3+2]‐Cycloaddition
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The intramolecular nitrile oxide olefin [3 + 2]‐cycloaddition offers a reliable entry toward 2‐aza‐bicyclo[4.3.0]‐nonanes, a substructure found in several bioactive piperidine alkaloids. The required oxime precursors were prepared in non‐racemic form by Ir‐catalyzed asymmetric allylation and ring‐closing metathesis. Options to transform the cycloaddition products into natural product‐related structures were also explored.
Studies toward the total synthesis of the piperidine alkaloid camporidine A are reported. The core ring system was established through intramolecular nitrile oxide olefin cycloaddition, while the precursors were assembled by Ir‐catalyzed asymmetric allylic amination in the presence of a chiral phosphoramidite ligand and subsequent Ru‐catalyzed ring‐closing metathesis. Using model compounds lacking the hexyl sidechain, we demonstrated that a 2‐aza‐bicyclo‐[4.3.0]nona‐5‐en‐7‐one intermediate, obtained from the isoxazoline cycloadduct by Raney–Nickel reduction, imine hydrolysis, and elimination, can be diastereoselectively epoxidized and further converted to camporidine analogs by Nicolaou–Baran dehydrogenation or E‐selective Wittig olefination. A related sequence was realized with n‐hexyl substituted compounds employing 2‐vinyl‐octylamine (98% ee), prepared by enantioselective allylation of ethyl nitroacetate and subsequent Krapcho decarboxylation and reduction. However, the epoxidation step failed in this case. An alternative route toward compounds displaying the core ring system of the target compounds started with the addition of 2‐(1,3‐dioxolan‐2‐yl)ethyl magnesium bromide to N‐methoxycarbonyl‐3‐(tri‐iso‐propylsilyl)pyridinium chloride to give a 1,2‐dihydropyridine derivative. Surprisingly, upon reaction of the acetal with hydroxylamine, the oxime intermediate directly underwent a formal intramolecular oxime olefin cycloaddition, which according to DFT calculations proceeded as a two‐step process. The resulting isoxazolidine was further transformed via bromo‐desilylation and Suzuki coupling to hexyl‐substituted derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
The intramolecular nitrile oxide olefin [3 + 2]-cycloaddition offers a reliable entry toward 2-aza-bicyclo[4.3.0]-nonanes, a substructure found in several bioactive piperidine alkaloids. The required oxime precursors were prepared in non-racemic form by Ir-catalyzed asymmetric allylation and ring-closing metathesis. Options to transform the cycloaddition products into natural product-related structures were also explored.


Studies toward the total synthesis of the piperidine alkaloid camporidine A are reported. The core ring system was established through intramolecular nitrile oxide olefin cycloaddition, while the precursors were assembled by Ir-catalyzed asymmetric allylic amination in the presence of a chiral phosphoramidite ligand and subsequent Ru-catalyzed ring-closing metathesis. Using model compounds lacking the hexyl sidechain, we demonstrated that a 2-aza-bicyclo-[4.3.0]nona-5-en-7-one intermediate, obtained from the isoxazoline cycloadduct by Raney–Nickel reduction, imine hydrolysis, and elimination, can be diastereoselectively epoxidized and further converted to camporidine analogs by Nicolaou–Baran dehydrogenation or E-selective Wittig olefination. A related sequence was realized with n-hexyl substituted compounds employing 2-vinyl-octylamine (98% ee), prepared by enantioselective allylation of ethyl nitroacetate and subsequent Krapcho decarboxylation and reduction. However, the epoxidation step failed in this case. An alternative route toward compounds displaying the core ring system of the target compounds started with the addition of 2-(1,3-dioxolan-2-yl)ethyl magnesium bromide to N-methoxycarbonyl-3-(tri-iso-propylsilyl)pyridinium chloride to give a 1,2-dihydropyridine derivative. Surprisingly, upon reaction of the acetal with hydroxylamine, the oxime intermediate directly underwent a formal intramolecular oxime olefin cycloaddition, which according to DFT calculations proceeded as a two-step process. The resulting isoxazolidine was further transformed via bromo-desilylation and Suzuki coupling to hexyl-substituted derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Alicia Köcher, Tobias Wilczek, Nora Katharina Korber, Jörg-Martin Neudörfl, Bernd Goldfuss, Hans-Günther Schmalz
item.dc:title: Synthesis of 2‐Aza‐Bicyclo[4.3.0]nonane Derivatives Related to the Camporidines via Intramolecular [3+2]‐Cycloaddition

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Redox Properties of Catalytically Generated Iminium Ions From α,β‐Unsaturated Aldehydes and Chiral Secondary Amines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We present a rapid, operationally simple, and broadly applicable protocol for the catalytic formation and in situ electrochemical characterization of chiral a,b‐unsaturated iminium ions by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Using only 5 mol% of the aminocatalyst, we systematically measure the reduction potential of over 50 iminium intermediates, revealing how catalyst structure, substrate identity and solvent influence their redox properties.
Chiral iminium ions are key catalytic intermediates in aminocatalysis, enabling a wide range of enantioselective transformations. Yet, a systematic study of how their electrochemical properties vary as a function of the different reaction components has remained elusive, largely due to their transient nature and inherent instability. Herein we show a general protocol for the catalytic generation and in situ cyclic voltammetric characterisation of over 50 chiral iminium ions. This analysis reveals how the catalyst electronics, aldehyde nature and substitution, and the solvent environment governs their reduction potential. This electrochemical mapping provides predictive insights into iminium ion reactivity via single‐electron transfer, laying the groundwork for the rational design of novel radical‐based asymmetric transformations.
item.content:encoded:
We present a rapid, operationally simple, and broadly applicable protocol for the catalytic formation and in situ electrochemical characterization of chiral a,b-unsaturated iminium ions by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Using only 5 mol% of the aminocatalyst, we systematically measure the reduction potential of over 50 iminium intermediates, revealing how catalyst structure, substrate identity and solvent influence their redox properties.


Chiral iminium ions are key catalytic intermediates in aminocatalysis, enabling a wide range of enantioselective transformations. Yet, a systematic study of how their electrochemical properties vary as a function of the different reaction components has remained elusive, largely due to their transient nature and inherent instability. Herein we show a general protocol for the catalytic generation and in situ cyclic voltammetric characterisation of over 50 chiral iminium ions. This analysis reveals how the catalyst electronics, aldehyde nature and substitution, and the solvent environment governs their reduction potential. This electrochemical mapping provides predictive insights into iminium ion reactivity via single-electron transfer, laying the groundwork for the rational design of novel radical-based asymmetric transformations.
item.dc:creator: Martí Gisbert, Paula Rodríguez, Xavier Companyó
item.dc:title: Redox Properties of Catalytically Generated Iminium Ions From α,β‐Unsaturated Aldehydes and Chiral Secondary Amines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Isoquinolones in Polycyclic Alkaloids: Structures, Bioactivities, and Synthetic Strategies
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Ring‐fused isoquinolone alkaloids exhibit attractive structural diversity and possess important pharmacological activities, ranging from anticancer to neuroprotection. Herein, we provide a comprehensive review of the synthesis of isoquinolone‐containing alkaloids, highlighting the various cyclization strategies employed, hoping to attract attention from both the synthetic and medicinal chemistry communities.
Isoquinolone is an important heterocycle and key pharmacophore in numerous bioactive molecules, including natural products and small‐molecule drugs. The fusion of the isoquinolone core with other cyclic skeletons generates significant structural diversity, exemplified by oxyberberine‐, nauclea‐, rosettacin‐, and oxynitidine‐type alkaloids, as well as clinically investigated small‐molecule inhibitors, such as indotecan (LMP400) and indimitecan (LMP776). This review systematically discusses the synthesis of ring‐fused isoquinolones, with a particular focus on natural products containing these scaffolds. The organization is based on key transformative reactions, including photo and radical cyclization, base‐mediated cyclization, and transition‐metal‐catalyzed cross‐coupling/C–H activation. By providing a unified discussion of diverse isoquinolone alkaloids and drugs, this review aims to draw attention to advances in synthetic methodologies and the structural diversity inherent to the isoquinolone core, thereby encouraging future investigations into isoquinolone‐based drug discovery.
item.content:encoded:
Ring-fused isoquinolone alkaloids exhibit attractive structural diversity and possess important pharmacological activities, ranging from anticancer to neuroprotection. Herein, we provide a comprehensive review of the synthesis of isoquinolone-containing alkaloids, highlighting the various cyclization strategies employed, hoping to attract attention from both the synthetic and medicinal chemistry communities.


Isoquinolone is an important heterocycle and key pharmacophore in numerous bioactive molecules, including natural products and small-molecule drugs. The fusion of the isoquinolone core with other cyclic skeletons generates significant structural diversity, exemplified by oxyberberine-, nauclea-, rosettacin-, and oxynitidine-type alkaloids, as well as clinically investigated small-molecule inhibitors, such as indotecan (LMP400) and indimitecan (LMP776). This review systematically discusses the synthesis of ring-fused isoquinolones, with a particular focus on natural products containing these scaffolds. The organization is based on key transformative reactions, including photo and radical cyclization, base-mediated cyclization, and transition-metal-catalyzed cross-coupling/C–H activation. By providing a unified discussion of diverse isoquinolone alkaloids and drugs, this review aims to draw attention to advances in synthetic methodologies and the structural diversity inherent to the isoquinolone core, thereby encouraging future investigations into isoquinolone-based drug discovery.
item.dc:creator: Qing Li, Liyuan Su, Xinqi Liu, Chengyang Yu, Xiaomei Wang, Qingfang Li, Di Zhang, Tianyu Wang, Xiangyu Zhang
item.dc:title: Isoquinolones in Polycyclic Alkaloids: Structures, Bioactivities, and Synthetic Strategies

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Cyclodesulfurization Strategy: Synthesis of 2‐Aminoquinazolines and 2‐Aminobenzimidazoles and Investigation of Reaction Mechanism Through Density functional Theory Calculations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We unveil a sustainable electrochemical strategy that enables one‐step synthesis of 2‐aminoquinazolines and 2‐aminobenzimidazoles via TBAI‐mediated cyclodesulfurization reaction. The process proceeds through iodine radical intermediates, offering broad scope, high yields, and strong experimental‐computational mechanistic validation.
A streamlined electrochemical protocol has been established that enables direct access to a diverse library of 2‐aminoquinazoline and 2‐aminobenzimidazole derivatives. This transformation proceeds via a tetrabutylammonium iodide (TBAI)‐mediated cyclodesulfurization reaction of various isothiocyanates with 2‐aminobenzylamine or o‐phenylenediamine, forming intermolecular CN bonds in a single step and delivering the heterocycles in yields up to 86%. The reaction proceeds through a radical pathway in which iodine radicals play a pivotal role in promoting cyclization, while molecular iodine itself does not function as a conventional catalyst. This distinction highlights the unique redox behavior of iodide under electrochemical conditions, where anodic oxidation generates reactive iodine species that transiently drive bond formation without persisting as active catalytic species. The robustness of this methodology is underscored by its broad scope, compatibility with sensitive functional groups, and the absence of transition metals or external oxidants, which makes it operationally simple. Mechanistic understanding has been deepened through cyclic voltammetry, radical inhibition assays, HRMS, and electricity on/off control experiments, which confirm the radical nature and the crucial role of electricity in this process. Furthermore, density functional theory calculations provide strong computational support for the proposed intermediates and mechanistic pathways.
item.content:encoded:
We unveil a sustainable electrochemical strategy that enables one-step synthesis of 2-aminoquinazolines and 2-aminobenzimidazoles via TBAI-mediated cyclodesulfurization reaction. The process proceeds through iodine radical intermediates, offering broad scope, high yields, and strong experimental-computational mechanistic validation.


A streamlined electrochemical protocol has been established that enables direct access to a diverse library of 2-aminoquinazoline and 2-aminobenzimidazole derivatives. This transformation proceeds via a tetrabutylammonium iodide (TBAI)-mediated cyclodesulfurization reaction of various isothiocyanates with 2-aminobenzylamine or o-phenylenediamine, forming intermolecular CN bonds in a single step and delivering the heterocycles in yields up to 86%. The reaction proceeds through a radical pathway in which iodine radicals play a pivotal role in promoting cyclization, while molecular iodine itself does not function as a conventional catalyst. This distinction highlights the unique redox behavior of iodide under electrochemical conditions, where anodic oxidation generates reactive iodine species that transiently drive bond formation without persisting as active catalytic species. The robustness of this methodology is underscored by its broad scope, compatibility with sensitive functional groups, and the absence of transition metals or external oxidants, which makes it operationally simple. Mechanistic understanding has been deepened through cyclic voltammetry, radical inhibition assays, HRMS, and electricity on/off control experiments, which confirm the radical nature and the crucial role of electricity in this process. Furthermore, density functional theory calculations provide strong computational support for the proposed intermediates and mechanistic pathways.
item.dc:creator: Kamal Kant, Th. Anjela Devi, Ahanthem Sonia, Singamayum Rabia, Ronaldo Nongmaithem, Gracy Salam, Priyadarshini Naik, Sourav Majumdar, Chandi C. Malakar, Reda A. Haggam
item.dc:title: Electrochemical Cyclodesulfurization Strategy: Synthesis of 2‐Aminoquinazolines and 2‐Aminobenzimidazoles and Investigation of Reaction Mechanism Through Density functional Theory Calculations

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Synthesis of Pyrimidine Thioesters Utilizing Diethyl 2,2′‐Disulfanediylbis(4‐methyl‐6‐phenylpyrimidine‐5‐carboxylate)
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This process is carried out under room temperature conditions and is excited by light emitted from a 10 W blue light‐emitting diode with a wavelength range from 430 to 435 nm, which then drives a photoinduced intermolecular reaction between diethyl 2,2'‐disulfanediylbis(4‐methyl‐6‐phenylpyrimidine‐5‐carboxylate) and various aldehyde compounds. This reaction system employs the organic photocatalyst 4CzIPN and proceeds smoothly in chloroform (TCM), ultimately achieving the efficient synthesis of corresponding pyrimidine thioester products with good isolated yields. Additionally, we have validated its reaction mechanism through density functional theory calculations.
Herein, we describe a metal‐free photosensitization process. This process is carried out under room‐temperature conditions and is excited by light emitted from a 10 W blue light‐emitting diode with a wavelength range from 430 to 435 nm, which then drives a photoinduced intermolecular reaction between diethyl 2,2′‐disulfanediylbis(4‐methyl‐6‐phenylpyrimidine‐5‐carboxylate) and various aldehyde compounds. This reaction system employs the organic photocatalyst 4CzIPN and proceeds smoothly in chloroform (TCM), ultimately achieving the efficient synthesis of corresponding pyrimidine thioester products with good isolated yields. Additionally, we have validated its reaction mechanism through density functional theory calculations.
item.content:encoded:
This process is carried out under room temperature conditions and is excited by light emitted from a 10 W blue light-emitting diode with a wavelength range from 430 to 435 nm, which then drives a photoinduced intermolecular reaction between diethyl 2,2'-disulfanediylbis(4-methyl-6-phenylpyrimidine-5-carboxylate) and various aldehyde compounds. This reaction system employs the organic photocatalyst 4CzIPN and proceeds smoothly in chloroform (TCM), ultimately achieving the efficient synthesis of corresponding pyrimidine thioester products with good isolated yields. Additionally, we have validated its reaction mechanism through density functional theory calculations.


Herein, we describe a metal-free photosensitization process. This process is carried out under room-temperature conditions and is excited by light emitted from a 10 W blue light-emitting diode with a wavelength range from 430 to 435 nm, which then drives a photoinduced intermolecular reaction between diethyl 2,2′-disulfanediylbis(4-methyl-6-phenylpyrimidine-5-carboxylate) and various aldehyde compounds. This reaction system employs the organic photocatalyst 4CzIPN and proceeds smoothly in chloroform (TCM), ultimately achieving the efficient synthesis of corresponding pyrimidine thioester products with good isolated yields. Additionally, we have validated its reaction mechanism through density functional theory calculations.
item.dc:creator: Zhao‐Zhen Zhou, Wen‐Li Zhen, Ya‐Hong Liu, Dong‐Ping Chen, Ming Li, Guo‐Qing Sun, Fa‐Xiang Yang, Xue‐Liang Zhang, Zheng‐Jun Quan, Xi‐Cun Wang

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Toward Sustainable NHC‐Based Photosensitizers: Scalable Synthesis and Postfunctionalization of Fe, Co, and Mn Complexes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A practical, user‐friendly synthesis of an iron–NHC photosensitizer is presented, designed for reliability, scalability, and broad adoption. By identifying simple but critical factors often overlooked, this work delivers a reproducible large‐scale protocol accessible to nonexperts, extends to other metals, and enables straightforward late‐stage functionalization for diverse applications.
Iron‐NHC (N‐heterocyclic carbene) complexes hold strong promise as earth‐abundant alternatives to Ru‐ and Ir‐based photosensitizers, but their synthesis represents additional challenges. Here, we present a systematic study aimed at developing a robust, reproducible, and scalable protocol for the synthesis of [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+ that is suitable for researchers of all skill levels. By examining the effects of base, solvent, temperature, concentration, and iron source, we identified several previously overlooked parameters, including FeBr2 solubility, temperature‐dependent precipitation, and solvent quality, that significantly impact reaction outcome. Optimization revealed that low‐temperature addition of LiHMDS and dilute reaction conditions (0.02 M) afford consistently improved yields. Further adjustment of ligand and base stoichiometry enabled reliable scale‐up, delivering the target complex in 64% yield on a 1 mmol scale. This practical procedure provides broad, dependable access to [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+, facilitating its use across catalysis, materials chemistry, and related fields. Furthermore, we show postfunctionalization routes of [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+ via Suzuki‐type coupling and expand the reaction scope to include additional transition metals such as Mn(IV) and Co(III).
item.content:encoded:
A practical, user-friendly synthesis of an iron–NHC photosensitizer is presented, designed for reliability, scalability, and broad adoption. By identifying simple but critical factors often overlooked, this work delivers a reproducible large-scale protocol accessible to nonexperts, extends to other metals, and enables straightforward late-stage functionalization for diverse applications.


Iron-NHC (N-heterocyclic carbene) complexes hold strong promise as earth-abundant alternatives to Ru- and Ir-based photosensitizers, but their synthesis represents additional challenges. Here, we present a systematic study aimed at developing a robust, reproducible, and scalable protocol for the synthesis of [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+ that is suitable for researchers of all skill levels. By examining the effects of base, solvent, temperature, concentration, and iron source, we identified several previously overlooked parameters, including FeBr2 solubility, temperature-dependent precipitation, and solvent quality, that significantly impact reaction outcome. Optimization revealed that low-temperature addition of LiHMDS and dilute reaction conditions (0.02 M) afford consistently improved yields. Further adjustment of ligand and base stoichiometry enabled reliable scale-up, delivering the target complex in 64% yield on a 1 mmol scale. This practical procedure provides broad, dependable access to [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+, facilitating its use across catalysis, materials chemistry, and related fields. Furthermore, we show postfunctionalization routes of [Fe(Brphtmeimb)2]+ via Suzuki-type coupling and expand the reaction scope to include additional transition metals such as Mn(IV) and Co(III).
item.dc:creator: Katerina Achilleos, Prabhat Ranjan, Ghena Baroudi, Felix Glaser, Abdullah M. Abudayyeh, Lakshmi S. Narayan, Thomas Thunissen, Koen Robeyns, Ludovic Troian‐Gautier
item.dc:title: Toward Sustainable NHC‐Based Photosensitizers: Scalable Synthesis and Postfunctionalization of Fe, Co, and Mn Complexes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper Nanoparticle‐Catalyzed Click Assembly of 1,2,3‐Triazolylphosphonates
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A series of 22 β‐1,2,3‐triazolylphosphonates (21 novel) was efficiently synthesized under green conditions by combining Cu(I)‐catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition click chemistry and organophosphorus methodologies. The compounds were fully characterized and evaluated as cholinesterase inhibitors, showing notable selectivity toward butyrylcholinesterase. Among them, 4ack stood out for its strong activity and significantly lower neurotoxicity, highlighting the potential of this scaffold for further exploration.
The development of efficient and sustainable synthetic methodologies for accessing structurally complex molecules remains a key objective in organic chemistry. Herein, we report the design and synthesis of a library of 22 β‐1,2,3‐triazolylphosphonate derivatives, 21 previously unreported, obtained through the integration of robust synthetic strategies under green conditions. The 1,2,3‐triazole scaffold was assembled via a Cu(I)‐catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition click reaction using supported copper nanoparticles as an efficient and recyclable catalyst, while the phosphonate fragments were introduced through established organophosphorus methodologies. All compounds were fully characterized and evaluated for their in vitro acetyl‐ and butyrylcholinesterase (AChE and BChE) inhibitory activity. The majority of the derivatives exhibited pronounced selectivity toward BChE inhibition, with compounds 4aca and 4ack emerging as the most potent inhibitors (IC50 = 2.7 and 2.9 µM, respectively). Notably, compound 4ack showed significantly lower neurotoxicity in N27 cells compared to 4aca, displaying a two‐order‐of‐magnitude separation between its BChE inhibitory potency and cytotoxic effects (IC50 = 255.3 and 37.3 µM, respectively). In silico absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion predictions were performed for all derivatives. Collectively, these results highlight β‐1,2,3‐triazolylphosphonates as a versatile chemical platform and underscore the effectiveness of combining click chemistry with organophosphorus approaches for the generation of structurally diverse bioactive candidates.
item.content:encoded:
A series of 22 β-1,2,3-triazolylphosphonates (21 novel) was efficiently synthesized under green conditions by combining Cu(I)-catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition click chemistry and organophosphorus methodologies. The compounds were fully characterized and evaluated as cholinesterase inhibitors, showing notable selectivity toward butyrylcholinesterase. Among them, 4ack stood out for its strong activity and significantly lower neurotoxicity, highlighting the potential of this scaffold for further exploration.


The development of efficient and sustainable synthetic methodologies for accessing structurally complex molecules remains a key objective in organic chemistry. Herein, we report the design and synthesis of a library of 22 β-1,2,3-triazolylphosphonate derivatives, 21 previously unreported, obtained through the integration of robust synthetic strategies under green conditions. The 1,2,3-triazole scaffold was assembled via a Cu(I)-catalyzed azide–alkyne cycloaddition click reaction using supported copper nanoparticles as an efficient and recyclable catalyst, while the phosphonate fragments were introduced through established organophosphorus methodologies. All compounds were fully characterized and evaluated for their in vitro acetyl- and butyrylcholinesterase (AChE and BChE) inhibitory activity. The majority of the derivatives exhibited pronounced selectivity toward BChE inhibition, with compounds 4aca and 4ack emerging as the most potent inhibitors (IC50 = 2.7 and 2.9 µM, respectively). Notably, compound 4ack showed significantly lower neurotoxicity in N27 cells compared to 4aca, displaying a two-order-of-magnitude separation between its BChE inhibitory potency and cytotoxic effects (IC50 = 255.3 and 37.3 µM, respectively). In silico absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion predictions were performed for all derivatives. Collectively, these results highlight β-1,2,3-triazolylphosphonates as a versatile chemical platform and underscore the effectiveness of combining click chemistry with organophosphorus approaches for the generation of structurally diverse bioactive candidates.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Ligand‐Enabled Pd‐Catalyzed Enantioselective C−H Amination for the Synthesis of Axially Chiral Biaryl Diamines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A new class of axially chiral biaryl diamine ligands possessing a binaphthyl skeleton was developed to promote the challenging Pd‐catalyzed enantioselective C−H amination of biaryl amine under very mild conditions. This protocol provides direct access to axially chiral biaryl diamine derivatives in up to 79% yield with up to 90.5:9.5 enantiomeric ratio (er).
Pd‐catalyzed enantioselective C−H amination of biaryl amines with morpholino benzoate has been achieved using a novel class of axially chiral diamine ligands featuring a binaphthyl skeleton. The reaction proceeds under very mild conditions and exhibits good functional group tolerance, delivering a range of axially chiral biaryl diamine derivatives bearing a morpholine unit in moderate to good yields (up to 79%) and with good enantioselectivity (up to 90.5:9.5 enantiomeric ratio (er)).
item.content:encoded:
A new class of axially chiral biaryl diamine ligands possessing a binaphthyl skeleton was developed to promote the challenging Pd-catalyzed enantioselective C−H amination of biaryl amine under very mild conditions. This protocol provides direct access to axially chiral biaryl diamine derivatives in up to 79% yield with up to 90.5:9.5 enantiomeric ratio (er).


Pd-catalyzed enantioselective C−H amination of biaryl amines with morpholino benzoate has been achieved using a novel class of axially chiral diamine ligands featuring a binaphthyl skeleton. The reaction proceeds under very mild conditions and exhibits good functional group tolerance, delivering a range of axially chiral biaryl diamine derivatives bearing a morpholine unit in moderate to good yields (up to 79%) and with good enantioselectivity (up to 90.5:9.5 enantiomeric ratio (er)).

item.dc:title: Ligand‐Enabled Pd‐Catalyzed Enantioselective C−H Amination for the Synthesis of Axially Chiral Biaryl Diamines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Darzens Reaction of 2,2,2‐Trifluoroacetophenones With α‐Fluoroindanones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Darzens condensations of 2,2,2‐trifluoroacetophenones with α‐fluoroindanones were developed. A variety of tetrasubstituted α, β‐epoxyketones were constructed using this protocol in moderate to good yields (up to 76%), with moderate to excellent diastereoselectivities (up to 94:6 dr).
Darzens condensations of 2,2,2‐trifluoroacetophenones with α‐fluoroindanones were developed. A variety of tetrasubstituted α,β‐epoxyketones were constructed using this protocol in moderate to good yields (up to 76%), with moderate to excellent diastereoselectivities (up to 94:6 dr).
item.content:encoded:
Darzens condensations of 2,2,2-trifluoroacetophenones with α-fluoroindanones were developed. A variety of tetrasubstituted α, β-epoxyketones were constructed using this protocol in moderate to good yields (up to 76%), with moderate to excellent diastereoselectivities (up to 94:6 dr).

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chemoenzymatic Synthesis of 5,11‐Epoxy‐4‐Hydroxyamoyphane, 4,11‐Epoxy‐5‐Hydroxyamoyphane and Their Structural Reassignment
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Chemoenzymatic synthesis of two sesquiterpene ethers was achieved featuring a heterologous synthesis of amorphane skeleton and a biomimetic epoxide ring‐opening process. The originally proposed tetrahydropyran containing structure was revised to 5,11‐epoxy‐4‐hydroxyamoyphane containing a tetrahydrofuran ring on the basis of X‐ray diffraction analysis of its para‐nitrobenzoyl ester derivative.
A chemoenzymatic synthesis of both 4,11‐epoxy‐5‐hydroxyamoyphane (1) and 5,11‐epoxy‐4‐hydroxyamoyphane (2) was achieved. The amorpha‐4,11‐diene containing the amorphane skeleton was obtained in 100 mg/L by supplementing prenol/isoprenol to an engineered E. coli harboring the isopentenol utilization pathway and amorpha‐4,11‐diene synthase (ADS). Selective epoxidation and hydration of the two double bonds of amorpha‐4,11‐diene, followed by a biomimetic epoxide ring‐opening process, allowed the synthesis of both 1 and 2. The structure of 2, containing the tetrahydrofuran ring, was further confirmed by X‐ray crystal diffraction analysis of its para‐nitrobenzoyl derivative. The structure of the natural product isolated from Fabiana imbricata, originally elucidated as 1 containing a tetrahydropyran ring, was thus revised to 2.
item.content:encoded:
Chemoenzymatic synthesis of two sesquiterpene ethers was achieved featuring a heterologous synthesis of amorphane skeleton and a biomimetic epoxide ring-opening process. The originally proposed tetrahydropyran containing structure was revised to 5,11-epoxy-4-hydroxyamoyphane containing a tetrahydrofuran ring on the basis of X-ray diffraction analysis of its para-nitrobenzoyl ester derivative.


A chemoenzymatic synthesis of both 4,11-epoxy-5-hydroxyamoyphane (1) and 5,11-epoxy-4-hydroxyamoyphane (2) was achieved. The amorpha-4,11-diene containing the amorphane skeleton was obtained in 100 mg/L by supplementing prenol/isoprenol to an engineered E. coli harboring the isopentenol utilization pathway and amorpha-4,11-diene synthase (ADS). Selective epoxidation and hydration of the two double bonds of amorpha-4,11-diene, followed by a biomimetic epoxide ring-opening process, allowed the synthesis of both 1 and 2. The structure of 2, containing the tetrahydrofuran ring, was further confirmed by X-ray crystal diffraction analysis of its para-nitrobenzoyl derivative. The structure of the natural product isolated from Fabiana imbricata, originally elucidated as 1 containing a tetrahydropyran ring, was thus revised to 2.
item.dc:creator: Tao Wang, Kaibiao Wang, Zhengren Xu
item.dc:title: Chemoenzymatic Synthesis of 5,11‐Epoxy‐4‐Hydroxyamoyphane, 4,11‐Epoxy‐5‐Hydroxyamoyphane and Their Structural Reassignment

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Lithium Diisopropylamide‐Mediated Ring‐Opening of Tetrahydrofuran in the Synthesis of Polyhydroxylated Indolizidines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Chiral tri‐ and pentahydroxy spirocyclopentane‐indolizidines were synthesized using lithium diisopropylamide “LDA” promoted diallylation and tetrahydrofuran “THF” ring‐opening in a one‐pot procedure as a key step. The sequence leading to the targeted hydroxy‐spiroindolizidines was achieved by cis‐dihydroxylation followed by reduction of the enamine and lactam in the precursors.
A synthesis of novel hydroxyethyl‐substituted tri‐ and penta‐hydroxylated spiro[cyclopentane‐indolizidines] has been reported, featuring an interesting lithium diisopropylamide “LDA”‐mediated α‐diallylation and tetrahydrofuran “THF” ring opening in one‐pot procedure as the key transformation. The synthetic sequence explored herein uses an enantiopure tricyclic lactam as a common chiral precursor, prepared on a gram scale from cost‐effective basic reagents. The key steps in this synthetic way included diastereoselective dihydroxylation reaction, ring‐closing ene–ene metathesis, enamine reduction, and finally lactam reduction. These transformations enabled the efficient and stereoselective construction of the ultimate polyhydroxy‐indolizidine scaffolds. As an application, the biological activity of the synthesized spiro‐indolizidines was evaluated against a panel of α‐glucosidases.
item.content:encoded:
Chiral tri- and pentahydroxy spirocyclopentane-indolizidines were synthesized using lithium diisopropylamide “LDA” promoted diallylation and tetrahydrofuran “THF” ring-opening in a one-pot procedure as a key step. The sequence leading to the targeted hydroxy-spiroindolizidines was achieved by cis-dihydroxylation followed by reduction of the enamine and lactam in the precursors.


A synthesis of novel hydroxyethyl-substituted tri- and penta-hydroxylated spiro[cyclopentane-indolizidines] has been reported, featuring an interesting lithium diisopropylamide “LDA”-mediated α-diallylation and tetrahydrofuran “THF” ring opening in one-pot procedure as the key transformation. The synthetic sequence explored herein uses an enantiopure tricyclic lactam as a common chiral precursor, prepared on a gram scale from cost-effective basic reagents. The key steps in this synthetic way included diastereoselective dihydroxylation reaction, ring-closing ene–ene metathesis, enamine reduction, and finally lactam reduction. These transformations enabled the efficient and stereoselective construction of the ultimate polyhydroxy-indolizidine scaffolds. As an application, the biological activity of the synthesized spiro-indolizidines was evaluated against a panel of α-glucosidases.
item.dc:creator: Paula Fraňová, Peter Šafář, Marchalín Štefan, Petra Pažitná, Ivana Žídeková, Ján Moncol, Adam Daïch
item.dc:title: Lithium Diisopropylamide‐Mediated Ring‐Opening of Tetrahydrofuran in the Synthesis of Polyhydroxylated Indolizidines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed N‐Alkenylation of Azidoformates With Alkenyl Boronic Acids: Synthesis of N‐Alkenylcarbamates
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A CuCl‐catalyzed N‐alkenylation of azidoformates with diverse alkenyl boronic acids is described. The transformation proceeds under mild, ligand‐free conditions in air at room temperature and accommodates styryl, α‐substituted, π‐extended, and cyclic substrates. Control experiments support a nitrene‐type pathway, providing efficient access to N‐alkenylcarbamates.
We report a copper‐catalyzed N‐alkenylation of azidoformates with a range of alkenyl boronic acids, providing an efficient and practical method for the synthesis of N‐alkenylcarbamates. The reaction proceeds at room temperature in an open flask using CuCl as the sole catalyst, without the need for added base or ligand. A broad spectrum of alkenyl boronic acids, including styryl, α‐substituted, cyclic, and heteroaryl derivatives, couples smoothly with diverse azidoformates to afford the corresponding enecarbamates in good to excellent yields. The method is operationally simple and readily scalable. Representative downstream transformations further demonstrate the synthetic utility of the products. Control experiments are consistent with the involvement of a nitrene‐type intermediate in the reaction pathway.
item.content:encoded:
A CuCl-catalyzed N-alkenylation of azidoformates with diverse alkenyl boronic acids is described. The transformation proceeds under mild, ligand-free conditions in air at room temperature and accommodates styryl, α-substituted, π-extended, and cyclic substrates. Control experiments support a nitrene-type pathway, providing efficient access to N-alkenylcarbamates.


We report a copper-catalyzed N-alkenylation of azidoformates with a range of alkenyl boronic acids, providing an efficient and practical method for the synthesis of N-alkenylcarbamates. The reaction proceeds at room temperature in an open flask using CuCl as the sole catalyst, without the need for added base or ligand. A broad spectrum of alkenyl boronic acids, including styryl, α-substituted, cyclic, and heteroaryl derivatives, couples smoothly with diverse azidoformates to afford the corresponding enecarbamates in good to excellent yields. The method is operationally simple and readily scalable. Representative downstream transformations further demonstrate the synthetic utility of the products. Control experiments are consistent with the involvement of a nitrene-type intermediate in the reaction pathway.
item.dc:creator: Jaehoon Lee, Ha‐Young Ryoo, Min‐Jung Lee, Inji Shin, Won‐Suk Kim
item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed N‐Alkenylation of Azidoformates With Alkenyl Boronic Acids: Synthesis of N‐Alkenylcarbamates

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis and Reactivity of Iodepinium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An efficient oxidative cyclization of (Z)‐iodostilbenes grants access to the long‐elusive class of seven‐membered diaryliodonium salts — the iodepinium triflates. X‐ray analysis reveals their distinctly nonplanar geometry, while reactivity studies uncover unprecedented pathways, including copper‐mediated conversion to phenanthrene and chalcogen‐induced ring contraction to 1‐iodophenanthrene.
Seven‐membered cyclic diaryliodonium salts, known as iodepinium salts, remain a significantly underexplored class of hypervalent iodine compounds. This scarcity is primarily attributed to the lack of efficient synthetic methodologies. Herein, we report an effective protocol for the synthesis of iodepinium triflates via the oxidative cyclization of iodinated (Z)‐stilbenes. In addition to establishing a substrate scope of 10 derivatives with yields up to 83%, the structural features of the unique seven‐membered ring were elucidated via X‐ray crystallography. Furthermore, initial investigations into their reactivity reveal unique decomposition and transformation pathways.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient oxidative cyclization of (Z)-iodostilbenes grants access to the long-elusive class of seven-membered diaryliodonium salts — the iodepinium triflates. X-ray analysis reveals their distinctly nonplanar geometry, while reactivity studies uncover unprecedented pathways, including copper-mediated conversion to phenanthrene and chalcogen-induced ring contraction to 1-iodophenanthrene.


Seven-membered cyclic diaryliodonium salts, known as iodepinium salts, remain a significantly underexplored class of hypervalent iodine compounds. This scarcity is primarily attributed to the lack of efficient synthetic methodologies. Herein, we report an effective protocol for the synthesis of iodepinium triflates via the oxidative cyclization of iodinated (Z)-stilbenes. In addition to establishing a substrate scope of 10 derivatives with yields up to 83%, the structural features of the unique seven-membered ring were elucidated via X-ray crystallography. Furthermore, initial investigations into their reactivity reveal unique decomposition and transformation pathways.
item.dc:creator: Jan Rick Koch, Mattis Damrath, Pim Puylaert, Boris J. Nachtsheim
item.dc:title: Synthesis and Reactivity of Iodepinium Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Achieving Diverse Functionalizations of Electron‐Deficient Aryl Halides via Carbonate‐Mediated Electron‐Donor–Acceptor Complexes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We report a simple and practical electron donor–acceptor complex‐mediated method that employs inexpensive carbonate bases to activate electron‐deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The protocol enables diverse transformations, including arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation, providing a versatile platform for the functionalization of aryl halide substrates.
Aryl halides are excellent precursors for functional group interconversion due to their malleability and diversity. They are also among the most common building blocks due to their use in cross‐coupling reactions. Traditional methodologies for activating these motifs make use of expensive precious metals, such as palladium, and often require the use of complex and even more expensive ligands. Electron‐donor–acceptor complexes have recently gained attention as a new photo‐induced approach for the activation of various molecular motifs, including aryl halides. Herein, further extension of these methodologies led to the development of a straightforward and simple protocol that uses inexpensive carbonate bases for the activation of electron‐deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The procedure was employed for the arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation of aryl halide starting materials.
item.content:encoded:
We report a simple and practical electron donor–acceptor complex-mediated method that employs inexpensive carbonate bases to activate electron-deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The protocol enables diverse transformations, including arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation, providing a versatile platform for the functionalization of aryl halide substrates.


Aryl halides are excellent precursors for functional group interconversion due to their malleability and diversity. They are also among the most common building blocks due to their use in cross-coupling reactions. Traditional methodologies for activating these motifs make use of expensive precious metals, such as palladium, and often require the use of complex and even more expensive ligands. Electron-donor–acceptor complexes have recently gained attention as a new photo-induced approach for the activation of various molecular motifs, including aryl halides. Herein, further extension of these methodologies led to the development of a straightforward and simple protocol that uses inexpensive carbonate bases for the activation of electron-deficient aryl iodides and bromides. The procedure was employed for the arylation, dehalogenation, deuteration, chalcogenation, and borylation of aryl halide starting materials.
item.dc:creator: Krishnakumar Sachidanandan, Cole Stenftenagel, Athul Joshy, Sébastien Laulhé
item.dc:title: Achieving Diverse Functionalizations of Electron‐Deficient Aryl Halides via Carbonate‐Mediated Electron‐Donor–Acceptor Complexes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Fast Reduction of Nitroalkenes Into Nitroalkanes Under Continuous‐Flow Chemical Conditions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Flow chemical synthesis was employed for the reduction of nitroalkenes to the corresponding nitroalkanes using sodium borohydride as the reducing agent. The reaction proceeds in only 150 s, affording products from good‐to‐excellent yields (61–96%).
Herein, we describe an efficient and rapid continuous‐flow reduction of nitroalkenes to nitroalkanes. The reaction, promoted by sodium borohydride at room temperature, delivered the corresponding nitroalkanes in good‐to‐excellent yields, tolerating a broad range of functionalities, with a remarkably short residence time of only 150 s.
item.content:encoded:
Flow chemical synthesis was employed for the reduction of nitroalkenes to the corresponding nitroalkanes using sodium borohydride as the reducing agent. The reaction proceeds in only 150 s, affording products from good-to-excellent yields (61–96%).


Herein, we describe an efficient and rapid continuous-flow reduction of nitroalkenes to nitroalkanes. The reaction, promoted by sodium borohydride at room temperature, delivered the corresponding nitroalkanes in good-to-excellent yields, tolerating a broad range of functionalities, with a remarkably short residence time of only 150 s.
item.dc:creator: Iqra Munir, Gianmarco Simonelli, Marino Petrini, Alessandro Palmieri
item.dc:title: Fast Reduction of Nitroalkenes Into Nitroalkanes Under Continuous‐Flow Chemical Conditions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Denitrogenative Sonogashira Coupling of Benzothiatriazine Dioxides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A palladium‐catalyzed denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides has been developed for highly efficient access to o‐alkynylbenzenesulfonamides under mild conditions. The protocol features a wide substrate scope, good functional group compatibility, and tunable reactivity by copper cocatalyst and tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.
A denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides catalyzed by 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2 or 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2/CuI for aryl or alkyl alkynes, respectively, has been developed for highly efficient synthesis of o‐alkynylbenzenesulfonamides. The scope of this protocol has been demonstrated by >30 examples, with yields of up to 98%, showing a good compatibility with common functional groups. In addition, a one‐pot denitrogenative alkynylation/5‐exo‐dig cyclization of N‐alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides with aryl alkynes is also described preliminarily under the palladium/copper bimetallic catalysis in the presence of tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.
item.content:encoded:
A palladium-catalyzed denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides has been developed for highly efficient access to o-alkynylbenzenesulfonamides under mild conditions. The protocol features a wide substrate scope, good functional group compatibility, and tunable reactivity by copper cocatalyst and tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.


A denitrogenative Sonogashira coupling of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides catalyzed by 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2 or 1 mol% PdCl2(PPh3)2/CuI for aryl or alkyl alkynes, respectively, has been developed for highly efficient synthesis of o-alkynylbenzenesulfonamides. The scope of this protocol has been demonstrated by >30 examples, with yields of up to 98%, showing a good compatibility with common functional groups. In addition, a one-pot denitrogenative alkynylation/5-exo-dig cyclization of N-alkyl benzothiatriazine dioxides with aryl alkynes is also described preliminarily under the palladium/copper bimetallic catalysis in the presence of tetrabutyl ammonium bromide (TBAB) additive.
item.dc:creator: Yuxian Wang, Shuyue Xiao, Gang Zou
item.dc:title: Palladium‐Catalyzed Denitrogenative Sonogashira Coupling of Benzothiatriazine Dioxides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Isotellurourea‐Catalyzed Enantioselective (4+2)‐Heterocycloadditions of But‐2‐ynoates and Michael Acceptors
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Isotelluroureas allow for the use of alkynoates for asymmetric (4+2)‐heterocycloadditions with different Michael acceptors.
Alk‐2‐ynoates are readily available starting materials which, in principle, can be utilized for cycloadditions in analogy to structurally similar allenoates. However, their significantly lower reactivity makes activation by established isothiourea and isoselenourea Lewis base catalysts difficult. Capitalizing on the higher nucleophilicity and basicity of our recently introduced class of isotellurourea catalysts we now succeeded in successfully employing alk‐2‐ynoates for asymmetric (4 + 2)‐heterocycloadditions as well, resulting in even improved enantioselectivities as compared to the use of analogous allenoates. These results demonstrate the high potential of the highly reactive isotelluroureas for the catalytic activation of less reactive starting materials where established catalyst classes come to their limits.
item.content:encoded:
Isotelluroureas allow for the use of alkynoates for asymmetric (4+2)-heterocycloadditions with different Michael acceptors.


Alk-2-ynoates are readily available starting materials which, in principle, can be utilized for cycloadditions in analogy to structurally similar allenoates. However, their significantly lower reactivity makes activation by established isothiourea and isoselenourea Lewis base catalysts difficult. Capitalizing on the higher nucleophilicity and basicity of our recently introduced class of isotellurourea catalysts we now succeeded in successfully employing alk-2-ynoates for asymmetric (4 + 2)-heterocycloadditions as well, resulting in even improved enantioselectivities as compared to the use of analogous allenoates. These results demonstrate the high potential of the highly reactive isotelluroureas for the catalytic activation of less reactive starting materials where established catalyst classes come to their limits.
item.dc:creator: Anna Scheucher, Mario Waser
item.dc:title: Isotellurourea‐Catalyzed Enantioselective (4+2)‐Heterocycloadditions of But‐2‐ynoates and Michael Acceptors

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrophilic Iodination of Heterocyclic Compounds. Recent Advances 2008–2025: Part II
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Electrophilic iodination using molecular iodine and iodide sources represents an efficient approach for accessing valuable iodinated organic compounds. This review highlights recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds from 2008 to 2025, emphasizing the role of heteroaryl iodides as important synthetic intermediates for biologically active molecules, cross‐coupling chemistry, radical reactions, pharmaceutical development, and medical imaging.
The iodination of heterocyclic compounds has significant interest in synthetic organic chemistry, as it provides versatile and efficient pathways for the synthesis of a wide range of biologically active molecules. This review aims to highlight recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds using elemental iodine or iodide‐based reagents, covering developments reported over the past 17 years.
item.content:encoded:
Electrophilic iodination using molecular iodine and iodide sources represents an efficient approach for accessing valuable iodinated organic compounds. This review highlights recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds from 2008 to 2025, emphasizing the role of heteroaryl iodides as important synthetic intermediates for biologically active molecules, cross-coupling chemistry, radical reactions, pharmaceutical development, and medical imaging.


The iodination of heterocyclic compounds has significant interest in synthetic organic chemistry, as it provides versatile and efficient pathways for the synthesis of a wide range of biologically active molecules. This review aims to highlight recent advances in the iodination of heterocyclic compounds using elemental iodine or iodide-based reagents, covering developments reported over the past 17 years.
